proposals.title: Efficient Pre-training via Multi-Modal Token Transfer and Progressive Stage Expansion
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm for achieving state-of-the-art performance in multimodal and language models relies on massive, computationally prohibitive pre-training from scratch. This creates significant barriers to research and deployment, limiting accessibility and increasing environmental impact. Exploring radically more efficient pre-training is crucial for democratizing advanced AI capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's core representational capacity can be efficiently bootstrapped by transferring learned token-level knowledge from a high-resource modality to a low-resource one, followed by a carefully staged expansion, achieving performance comparable to models trained with orders of magnitude more compute.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, inspired by Bridge-Coder, we will transfer capabilities from a large, pre-trained visual tokenizer (e.g., VQ-GAN) to initialize a text tokenizer and embedding layers for a language model, leveraging shared semantic structure. Second, we will apply a "Worse is Better" progressive training strategy, starting with a heavily compressed, low-fidelity token space and gradually increasing token bandwidth and model size in stages, optimizing the compression-generation tradeoff at each step to maximize learning efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for drastically reducing pre-training compute for multimodal and language models. It will yield empirical evidence on the transferability of token-level representations across modalities and the efficiency gains of progressive, staged training paradigms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the combined, systematic application of cross-modal token transfer and progressive stage expansion specifically to reduce pre-training compute, moving beyond isolated efficiency improvements in architecture or data curation.

proposals.title: Internalizing Knowledge: Training for Perfect Recall to Eliminate the Need for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has become a dominant paradigm for grounding large language models in external knowledge, mitigating hallucinations and improving factual accuracy. However, RAG introduces significant latency, complexity, and dependency on curated corpora. This work questions the core assumption that external retrieval is always necessary, proposing instead to push the limits of a model's inherent capacity to internalize and perfectly recall knowledge during training, thereby enabling high-fidelity generation without retrieval.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically structuring training data and employing a staged, multimodal curriculum that emphasizes dense knowledge integration, a model can learn to internalize a comprehensive knowledge base with near-perfect recall, rendering retrieval mechanisms superfluous for generation tasks within its trained domain.
proposals.method: We will design a staged training pipeline. First, we will train a base model on a massive, diverse corpus of image-text pairs and long-context documents, using next-token prediction. Second, we will introduce a 'knowledge consolidation' stage using a novel objective that interleaves factual QA, cross-modal grounding (e.g., describing images from detailed captions), and counterfactual correction tasks, all within a single, extended sequence. This forces the model to compress and relate information across modalities and tokens. We will leverage techniques from recent visual tokenization work (e.g., 'When Worse is Better') to create efficient, high-fidelity multimodal representations as inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete upper-bound analysis on the internal knowledge capacity of transformer-based multimodal models. It will deliver a novel training framework for knowledge-intensive models that reduces system complexity. The findings will critically inform the trade-off between model parameterization, training data design, and the necessity of external retrieval systems.
proposals.novelty: While existing work focuses on improving retrieval or integrating retrieved data, this proposal inverts the problem, aiming to engineer training that makes retrieval obsolete for specific knowledge domains. It uniquely combines a staged training curriculum with multimodal knowledge fusion objectives explicitly designed for perfect internal recall, moving beyond standard language modeling loss.

proposals.title: Continuous Visual Embeddings: A Unified Language Model Framework for Image and Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Current unified language models for vision and language rely on discrete image tokenization (e.g., VQ-VAE, VQ-GAN), which incurs information loss and a compression-generation tradeoff, as noted in recent work. This discretization creates a representational mismatch with the continuous nature of text embeddings, potentially limiting model expressivity and generation quality. Investigating continuous representations could bridge this gap, enabling more seamless and higher-fidelity multimodal understanding and generation.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing discrete visual tokens with a continuous, learnable embedding space within a unified autoregressive language model framework will improve the model's ability to capture fine-grained visual details and lead to superior performance in conditional image generation and multimodal reasoning tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt a decoder-only Transformer architecture, similar to those used for large language models, to process sequences of continuous vectors. For images, we will replace a discrete tokenizer with a CNN or ViT-based encoder that outputs a sequence of continuous feature vectors, which are then projected into the model's embedding space. A key technical challenge is training stability; we will employ techniques like pre-training the visual encoder with a reconstruction loss, followed by joint fine-tuning with a next-token prediction objective over interleaved sequences of text and continuous visual embeddings. We will benchmark against discrete-token baselines (e.g., models using VQ-GAN) on tasks like text-to-image generation and visual question answering.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical comparison of continuous versus discrete visual representations within a unified generative framework. It will yield a novel model architecture capable of directly generating and reasoning over continuous visual features, potentially setting a new direction for multimodal model design. The findings will directly inform the fundamental trade-offs in visual representation for generative AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored continuous latent spaces in VAEs or diffusion models, integrating a *continuous* visual sequence as a first-class citizen within a standard next-token-prediction language model is largely unexplored. This approach directly challenges the prevailing assumption that discrete quantization is necessary for integrating vision into autoregressive models.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Multimodal Instruction Following: The Role of Model Size and Data Diversity in Unified Vision-Language Tasks
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws for language models are well-established, but their applicability to multimodal models combining vision and language is less understood, particularly for instruction-following tasks. As models like GPT-4V and Gemini push the boundaries of multimodal reasoning, it remains unclear how performance on complex, open-ended tasks scales with model parameters and the diversity of instruction-tuning data, a critical gap for efficient resource allocation in future model development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling model size and instruction-tuning data diversity for a unified multimodal architecture will yield superlinear performance gains on complex, compositional vision-language tasks, but that the marginal benefit of data diversity will saturate faster than that of model parameters.
proposals.method: We will extend a Transformer-based, modality-agnostic architecture (inspired by FLAVA and CM3) that processes image and text via a shared token space. We will systematically scale the model from 1B to 30B parameters and create instruction-tuning datasets of varying size and diversity, mixing existing datasets (e.g., VQA-v2, GQA, NLVR2) with synthetically generated instructions. Performance will be evaluated on a novel benchmark of compositional tasks requiring cross-modal reasoning and instruction fidelity, measuring scaling coefficients for both scaling axes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical scaling laws for multimodal instruction-tuning, guiding compute-optimal training strategies for future models. It will also release a new benchmark for evaluating compositional multimodal understanding and a large-scale, diverse instruction-tuning dataset.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws exist for unimodal models, this study uniquely investigates the interaction between model scale and data diversity specifically for instruction-tuned multimodal systems, moving beyond simple classification to open-ended generation and reasoning.

proposals.title: Unified Modal-Aware Retrieval for Cross-Modal Generation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has proven effective for language tasks, but its application to multimodal contexts (e.g., text-to-image, image captioning) remains limited. Current methods often rely on separate, modality-specific retrievers or naive joint embeddings, which fail to capture the nuanced, asymmetric relationships between modalities. This inefficiency hinders the quality and coherence of generated outputs in tasks requiring cross-modal understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a retrieval mechanism trained with a unified, modal-aware contrastive objective over a shared token space will significantly improve the relevance and utility of retrieved multimodal contexts, thereby enhancing the performance of downstream multimodal generation models.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage training framework. First, we will train a unified encoder to project image patches (via a ViT) and text tokens into a shared embedding space, using a novel modal-aware contrastive loss that weights positive pairs based on task-specific alignment (e.g., captioning vs. VQA). Second, we will integrate this retriever with a multimodal decoder (e.g., a conditioned transformer) in a retrieval-augmented pipeline, where retrieved multimodal tokens from a large-scale corpus are fused with input queries using cross-attention. We will evaluate on tasks like open-domain visual question answering and text-conditioned image generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, general-purpose retrieval mechanism for multimodal tasks, improving generation fidelity and factual grounding. It will also contribute a new benchmark for evaluating cross-modal retrieval quality in generative settings, moving beyond static retrieval metrics.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats modalities separately or uses simple fusion, our method explicitly models the asymmetry and task-dependence of cross-modal relationships within a unified retrieval architecture, directly optimizing for retrieval utility in generative downstream tasks.

proposals.title: Contrastive Decoding for Multimodal Coherence: Improving Generation in Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Multimodal models like Flamingo, BLIP-2, and LLaVA often generate plausible but generic or unfaithful outputs by relying on strong language priors, ignoring visual context. Contrastive decoding, which sharpens generation by contrasting distributions from a strong 'expert' and a weak 'amateur' model, has shown promise in text-only LLMs for reducing repetition and improving factuality. Its application to multimodal architectures, where the 'amateur' could be a model component deprived of visual tokens, remains unexplored and could directly address the modality imbalance problem.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a self-contained, contrastive decoding objective during inference to multimodal encoder-decoder and decoder-only models will significantly improve the visual grounding and specificity of generated text without additional training. We hypothesize that the contrast between a full model (expert) and a model ablated of visual input (amateur) will suppress language-only hallucinations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the contrastive decoding algorithm to multimodal settings. For models like LLaVA and BLIP-2, we will define the 'expert' as the full model processing both image tokens and text, and the 'amateur' as an identical model where image tokens are masked or replaced with a null embedding. The generation probability distribution will be sharpened by subtracting the amateur's logits, scaled by an adaptive penalty term. We will systematically evaluate this on VQA, captioning, and instruction-following benchmarks (e.g., VQAv2, NoCaps, MME), measuring metrics like CLIPScore and human evaluation for hallucination rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, training-free inference method to enhance the visual faithfulness of multimodal generation. It will yield a systematic analysis of how contrastive decoding transfers across different multimodal architectures (encoder-decoder vs. decoder-only) and tokenization schemes. The resulting code and benchmarks will establish a new baseline for efficient, post-hoc improvement of existing vision-language models.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive decoding is established in NLP, its application to multimodal models is novel. The key innovation is framing the visual modality ablation as the 'amateur' model, creating a direct contrast for suppressing language-only generation. This approach is distinct from fine-tuning or architectural changes, offering a plug-and-play inference-time enhancement.

proposals.title: Sparse MoE for Efficient Scaling of Multimodal Token-Based Models
proposals.motivation: Scaling dense multimodal models (e.g., vision-language models) is computationally prohibitive, as all parameters are activated for every input. Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have shown promise in language models for efficient scaling by sparsely activating sub-networks, but their application to multimodal domains remains underexplored. This work addresses the critical need for more parameter-efficient training and inference in models that process both image and text tokens.
proposals.hypothesis: A sparsely-gated Mixture-of-Experts architecture, applied to the joint multimodal representation space of a token-based model, can achieve superior performance-to-compute scaling compared to dense models of equivalent parameter count, without sacrificing multimodal alignment or generation quality.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer-based multimodal architecture (e.g., similar to Flamingo or a unified tokenizer model) by replacing dense feed-forward layers with MoE layers. Experts will be specialized for different input modalities and tasks, routed via a learned gating network over concatenated image and text token sequences. We will investigate routing strategies to balance load and encourage cross-modal expert specialization, building on pre-2026 MoE techniques like GShard and Switch Transformers. Training will leverage staged pre-training, first on modality-specific data and then on aligned multimodal corpora.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a blueprint for building vastly larger but computationally feasible multimodal models. It will yield empirical results on the scaling laws and expert specialization patterns in multimodal MoEs, and release open-source model architectures and training code to catalyze further research in efficient multimodal AI.
proposals.novelty: While MoE is established in unimodal NLP, its systematic integration and analysis for *joint* image-text token streams is novel. The key innovation is designing and routing experts to handle heterogeneous, interleaved multimodal token sequences, moving beyond simple modality-specific towers.

proposals.title: Token-Aligned Multimodal Distillation: Efficiently Compressing Vision-Language Models via a Unified Token Space
proposals.motivation: Large multimodal models (LMMs) like Flamingo or BLIP-2 achieve impressive performance but are computationally prohibitive for real-time applications. Knowledge distillation offers a path to efficiency, but existing methods struggle with the modality gap, often failing to effectively transfer cross-modal reasoning capabilities from teacher to student. This work addresses the critical need for deployable, high-performance vision-language assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distilling a large LMM into a smaller, unified transformer via a shared, semantically aligned token space for both visual and textual inputs will preserve cross-modal reasoning fidelity while drastically improving inference speed and memory footprint.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained, high-performance LMM as a static teacher. The student will be a transformer decoder initialized from a small language model (e.g., a 1-3B parameter variant). Building on insights from visual tokenization trade-offs, we will process images through a lightweight, frozen visual encoder (e.g., ViT-L) and project its output tokens into the student's textual embedding space. Distillation will be performed over multiple stages: first on image-text matching and captioning data to align modalities, then on generative tasks using a combination of output logit matching and intermediate feature alignment, specifically targeting the attention maps over the unified token sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a rigorously evaluated, efficient LMM suitable for deployment. It will provide a novel distillation framework and analysis of cross-modal knowledge transfer bottlenecks. The resulting model and code will serve as a strong baseline for efficient multimodal research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior distillation that treats modalities separately or distills only the language head, our method explicitly creates and distills into a unified token space, directly targeting the core cross-modal fusion mechanism. This bridges Aghajanyan's expertise in visual tokenization trade-offs and model capability transfer.

proposals.title: DenseCap-VQ: Bridging Visual Tokenization and Language Generation for Exhaustive Image Description
proposals.motivation: Current image captioning models produce short, generic descriptions, failing to capture the dense, compositional details necessary for applications like accessibility, visual question answering, and multimodal reasoning. This inverse problem of generating exhaustive text from a single image remains a significant challenge in multimodal AI. A solution would bridge the granularity gap between high-fidelity visual perception and coherent, long-form language generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-stage model, which first learns a dense, discrete visual tokenization of an image and then uses a large language model (LLM) to autoregressively generate text from these tokens, can produce more exhaustive and accurate descriptions than end-to-end captioning systems. The key is optimizing the visual tokenizer for the compression-generation tradeoff to preserve fine-grained details.
proposals.method: We propose a novel two-stage architecture. First, a high-capacity visual tokenizer (e.g., a modified VQ-GAN or VQ-VAE-2) will be trained to compress images into a dense grid of discrete tokens, explicitly balancing reconstruction fidelity with sequence length for language modeling, informed by recent work on the compression-generation tradeoff. Second, these visual tokens will be treated as a foreign language and fed into a pre-trained, frozen LLM (like LLaMA or OPT) using a lightweight, trainable 'bridge' adapter network. This adapter, inspired by parameter-efficient transfer methods, will map the visual token embeddings into the LLM's embedding space, enabling the LLM to generate dense captions autoregressively.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new state-of-the-art model for dense image captioning, measured by metrics like SPICE and novel coverage-based scores. It will provide a deeper understanding of how to structure visual information as a sequence for optimal consumption by LLMs. The proposed 'bridge' methodology offers a generalizable blueprint for injecting high-density visual information into frozen text generators.
proposals.novelty: While visual tokenization and LLM-based generation exist separately, their co-design for the specific task of *exhaustive* description is novel. The approach uniquely focuses on optimizing the first visual stage not just for reconstruction, but explicitly for the downstream language generation task, treating it as a transfer problem from a high-resource (vision) to a low-resource (dense caption) modality.

proposals.title: Contra-Code: Training for Code Comprehension via Contextual Span Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current code generation models, trained autoregressively, excel at producing syntactically valid code but often lack deep semantic understanding of code fragments within their broader programmatic context. This limits their performance on tasks like bug detection, code summarization, and program repair, which require robust comprehension. Inverting the training objective to predict context from a fragment could force models to learn richer, more structured representations of code semantics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a language model to predict the surrounding programmatic context (e.g., preceding/following lines, function signatures, imports) given a central code fragment will yield representations that significantly outperform standard autoregressive models on code comprehension benchmarks, while retaining competitive generative capabilities.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture (e.g., T5 or BART). For each code snippet in a large corpus (e.g., GitHub), we will mask a contiguous span of tokens and task the model with reconstructing the immediate context (e.g., 5-10 lines before and after). This 'span-to-context' objective inverts the standard causal masking. We will experiment with multi-modal conditioning, using visual tokens from code structure visualizations (inspired by 'When Worse is Better') to provide an additional signal. Training will be staged: first on the inverted objective, then fine-tuned on downstream tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel pre-training paradigm for code that prioritizes comprehension. It will deliver a model with state-of-the-art performance on code understanding tasks (e.g., CodeXGLUE's defect detection and clone detection). We will also release a new benchmark for evaluating the contextual grounding of code representations.
proposals.novelty: While masked language modeling exists, applying it in an inverted, 'span-to-context' formulation for code is novel. Combining this with a staged training approach and optional multi-modal visual tokens from code structure directly targets the comprehension-generation trade-off in a new way.

proposals.title: Massively Scaling Code Infilling: Training on a 10x Corpus to Unify Capabilities Across Programming Languages
proposals.motivation: Current code generation models are limited by the scale and diversity of their training data, particularly for low-resource programming languages. This data bottleneck constrains their ability to perform robust, language-agnostic infilling and understanding, which is critical for real-world developer tools. Scaling training data by an order of magnitude presents a clear, yet unexplored, path to breaking this limitation and achieving more general code intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a large-scale infilling model (e.g., a decoder-only transformer) on a 10x larger, multi-language code corpus will lead to emergent, unified capabilities across high- and low-resource languages, significantly improving performance on complex, cross-lingual code infilling and editing tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a massive, deduplicated corpus spanning mainstream and low-resource languages, scaling beyond existing datasets like The Stack. We will train a causal transformer model, adapted for infilling via a specialized masking strategy over code tokens, building on architectures like InCoder. The training will employ efficient, multi-stage scaling techniques, potentially leveraging mixture-of-experts (MoE) layers to manage the increased model capacity required to absorb the vast data. Evaluation will rigorously benchmark infilling accuracy and transfer learning on held-out languages.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the largest publicly available infilling-specialized code model, setting a new state-of-the-art for code completion and editing. It will provide empirical evidence on the data scaling laws for code models and demonstrate how massive data reduces the performance gap between high- and low-resource programming languages.
proposals.novelty: While scaling language models is common, systematically scaling a dedicated *infilling* model on code by an order of magnitude in data is novel. The focus is not merely on next-token prediction but on using scale to solve the specific challenge of unified, context-aware generation *within* existing code across diverse syntactic structures.

proposals.title: In-Context Coder: Generating Programmatic Context for Targeted Code Completion
proposals.motivation: Current code generation models excel at next-token prediction or infilling, but they struggle with the inverse problem: synthesizing the broader context (e.g., preceding function definitions, imports, class structures) that logically necessitates a given code snippet. This capability is crucial for tasks like intelligent code repair, where a bug fix must be integrated into a coherent program, and for educational tools that explain code by generating its likely use case. Addressing this inverse problem can bridge the gap between localized edits and holistic program understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multimodal encoder-decoder model, trained to treat a central code snippet and a natural language task description as conditional inputs, can accurately generate the surrounding functional and structural code context, thereby solving a structured inverse problem in program synthesis.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture (e.g., T5 or a decoder-only model like CodeGen with a prefix encoder). The model will take as input a concatenated sequence of a task description and a central code snippet, both tokenized. The training objective will be to autoregressively generate the code that precedes and follows the central snippet. We will train on a curated dataset from GitHub, creating examples by extracting code blocks and their surrounding context, formulating the task description via heuristics or a separate captioning model. Training will leverage techniques from Aghajanyan's prior work on bridging model capabilities and multimodal tokenization.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new task formulation for code generation—context generation from a core snippet—and provide a model capable of it. It will advance tools for automated software maintenance and pedagogy. The research will also yield insights into the representational trade-offs in encoding code for inverse synthesis tasks.
proposals.novelty: While most code LLMs generate *from* context, this work explicitly trains a model to generate the context *for* a given code piece, framing it as a conditional multimodal generation problem. This inverse approach is distinct from standard infilling and connects program synthesis to causal reasoning in code.

proposals.title: RustCoder: A High-Performance, Domain-Specialized Infilling Model for Rust
proposals.motivation: Generalist code generation models like Codex or CodeGen often underperform on complex, low-level languages like Rust, which have strict ownership rules and lifetimes. High-quality, context-aware infilling (e.g., completing a function body) is critical for developer productivity but remains a challenge for these languages. Specializing a model for a single language could unlock superior performance by deeply encoding its unique syntax and semantics, a direction less explored than multi-language training.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model, initialized from a strong generalist code model and then intensively trained and architecturally adapted specifically for Rust, will significantly outperform generalist and multi-lingual models on Rust-specific infilling tasks by better capturing its complex, non-local constraints.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained large language model (e.g., Code Llama) using a multi-stage training strategy. First, we will continue pre-training on a massive, deduplicated corpus of high-quality Rust code (e.g., from crates.io). Second, we will fine-tune for infilling using a causal masking objective over token sequences, but with a novel 'semantic span' masking strategy that prioritizes Rust-specific constructs like ownership blocks or unsafe sections. We will experiment with modal-specific adaptations, such as augmenting the tokenizer with dedicated tokens for Rust keywords and common library patterns, informed by prior work on visual tokenization trade-offs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a state-of-the-art, open-source infilling model for Rust, rigorously benchmarked against generalist models. It will provide a blueprint and analysis for language-specific specialization, detailing the trade-offs in data efficiency, architectural modifications, and performance gains. The findings will advance the field of domain-adaptive training for code.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Bridge-Coder transfers capabilities between languages, we focus on deep specialization for a single complex language, pushing performance boundaries. The novelty lies in the combined approach of Rust-specific corpus curation, semantic span masking for infilling, and tokenizer specialization, moving beyond simple continued pre-training of a generalist model.

proposals.title: Unified Infilling: Joint Training on Code and Natural Language for Cross-Modal Generalization
proposals.motivation: Current large language models are typically trained on separate corpora for code and natural language, limiting their ability to transfer structural reasoning from code to prose tasks and semantic understanding from prose to code generation. This siloed approach misses a key opportunity for cross-modal generalization, which is crucial for tasks like generating code from specifications or writing documentation from code. A unified training objective could create more versatile and data-efficient models.
proposals.hypothesis: Jointly training a transformer model on a unified infilling objective across code and natural language documents will improve performance on both code-specific and language-specific tasks compared to models trained on either modality alone, by fostering a shared representation of structure and semantics.
proposals.method: We will pre-train a decoder-only transformer from scratch on a mixed corpus of source code (from repositories like GitHub) and high-quality prose (e.g., Wikipedia, books). The core objective is a unified infilling task, where random spans of tokens are masked within both code and text documents, and the model must autoregressively reconstruct them. We will adapt techniques from Bridge-Coder for cross-lingual transfer and explore tokenization strategies informed by visual tokenization trade-offs to handle the distinct vocabularies. The training will be staged, potentially beginning with one modal domain before introducing the other to stabilize learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, publicly released model demonstrating improved cross-modal reasoning. It will provide empirical evidence for the benefits of joint code-language pre-training and a methodological framework for designing unified training objectives across disparate data modalities, advancing multimodal understanding beyond vision-language pairs.
proposals.novelty: While models like Codex or InCoder are trained on code infilling, and T5 on text infilling, a single model trained end-to-end on a deliberately mixed, interleaved stream of code and prose under a consistent infilling objective is unexplored. This moves beyond simple concatenation of datasets to a truly integrated training regimen.

proposals.title: Contrastive Alignment for Multimodal Generation: Improving Passage-Query Coherence with Self-Supervised Vision Losses
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal generation models, especially those trained on image-text pairs, often produce outputs where the generated text is only loosely aligned with the visual or contextual query, leading to hallucinations or generic descriptions. This misalignment limits their reliability in precise applications like visual question answering or document-grounded generation. Borrowing robust alignment techniques from self-supervised vision, where contrastive losses like InfoNCE have proven highly effective for learning invariant representations, offers a promising but underexplored direction for improving generative fidelity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a contrastive learning objective, inspired by self-supervised vision methods such as CLIP and SimCLR, into the training of a multimodal generative model will force a tighter latent alignment between input queries (e.g., images or passages) and generated text, thereby improving output coherence and reducing hallucination.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer-based multimodal architecture (e.g., a vision-language model similar to Flamingo or a text-to-text model with visual tokens) by adding a contrastive alignment stage during training. Specifically, we will compute a contrastive loss (InfoNCE) between the encoded representation of the input query (image or passage) and the encoded representation of the generated text, treating matched query-generation pairs as positives and in-batch others as negatives. This auxiliary loss will be combined with the standard autoregressive generation loss, following a multi-task training regime. We will experiment with different strategies for obtaining the generative text representation, such as using the decoder's final hidden state for the generated sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel training paradigm that directly optimizes for semantic alignment in generative tasks, moving beyond maximum likelihood estimation. It will provide empirical evidence on how contrastive objectives from self-supervised vision can transfer to generation, potentially establishing a new best practice for training coherent multimodal models. The resulting models should show measurable improvements in tasks requiring precise query-passage alignment.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established in representation learning and retrieval, its direct application as an auxiliary loss to train a conditional generative model for improved alignment is novel. This approach uniquely bridges the gap between discriminative contrastive methods from vision and the generative objectives dominant in language and multimodal models.

proposals.title: Distilling Generative Re-Rankers into Efficient Cross-Encoders for Multimodal Retrieval
proposals.motivation: In multimodal information retrieval, generative models (e.g., large language models) used as re-rankers achieve high accuracy by deeply understanding query-document relationships, but their slow inference speed is prohibitive for real-time applications. This creates a critical efficiency bottleneck in systems requiring low-latency retrieval over large-scale image-text corpora. A solution is needed to preserve the nuanced understanding of these powerful re-rankers while drastically improving throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rich, slow reasoning of a generative re-ranker (acting as a teacher) can be effectively distilled into a fast, lightweight cross-encoder model (the student), enabling near-state-of-the-art retrieval accuracy with orders-of-magnitude faster inference. This distillation will successfully transfer the teacher's ability to model fine-grained cross-modal interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage distillation framework. First, a large generative model (e.g., Flan-T5 or a small LLaMA variant) will generate soft relevance scores and, crucially, detailed textual rationales for query-document pairs. Second, a compact cross-encoder (e.g., a small BERT or RoBERTa model) will be trained not only on the scores but also on token-level targets derived from the teacher's hidden states and rationales, using a combined loss of KL divergence and masked language modeling. The student model will be optimized for efficient single-forward-pass inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, highly efficient cross-encoder architecture specifically trained for multimodal retrieval via generative distillation. It will provide a practical blueprint for compressing expensive generative re-ranking stages into deployable components, significantly advancing the state of efficient retrieval. We will validate our approach on established benchmarks like MS-COCO and Flickr30k.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge distillation for dense retrievers exists, distilling a *generative* re-ranker's nuanced, reasoning-based relevance judgments into a *cross-encoder* for multimodal retrieval is a novel direction. This approach uniquely leverages the teacher's generative capabilities to create a student that is both fast and deeply understanding, moving beyond simple score matching.

proposals.title: HyperMask: Sparse, Hypernetwork-Guided Adaptation from Unlabeled Multi-Task Pre-Finetuning
proposals.motivation: Few-shot learning remains a critical challenge, as large models often overfit or fail to generalize from minimal labeled data. While Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA and adapters reduce overfitting, they still rely on labeled data for the target task and can underutilize the rich, general features learned during pre-training. A method that first builds a more robust, general-purpose foundation model via unsupervised multi-task learning and then enables ultra-sparse, data-efficient specialization could significantly advance few-shot performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-stage approach—first performing self-supervised, multi-task pre-finetuning on diverse unlabeled data, followed by a hypernetwork that predicts an extremely sparse, task-specific mask and delta weights from few labels—will yield superior few-shot adaptation by maximizing feature utility while minimizing destructive interference.
proposals.method: We will first pre-finetune a base model (e.g., a Vision Transformer or T5) on a diverse corpus of unlabeled image-text pairs using self-supervised objectives like masked token modeling and contrastive learning. Subsequently, a lightweight hypernetwork (a small MLP) will be trained on the few labeled examples. This hypernetwork will take a task embedding as input and output: 1) a sparse binary mask selecting a critical subset of the pre-finetuned model's parameters, and 2) low-rank delta weights (inspired by LoRA) for only those selected parameters. The adaptation will be optimized with a standard cross-entropy loss, but updates will be applied strictly to the hypernetwork and the sparse, unmasked delta weights.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for data-efficient adaptation that cleanly separates unsupervised representation consolidation from label-driven specialization. It will provide empirical evidence on vision-language benchmarks that ultra-sparse, hypernetwork-guided updates outperform standard PEFT in few-shot settings. The method also offers a practical pathway for adapting large models to new domains with minimal labeling cost.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific sequence of unsupervised multi-task pre-finetuning followed by hypernetwork-guided *sparse masking* for adaptation. Unlike prior PEFT work that applies modifications uniformly or to fixed modules, our method learns to identify and update only the most task-critical parameters, guided by a hypernetwork conditioned on few-shot examples.

proposals.title: Taxonomic Pre-Finetuning: Hierarchical Task Manifold Mapping for Label-Efficient Transfer
proposals.motivation: Current transfer learning paradigms, while powerful, often require significant labeled data for pre-finetuning on a target task's domain. This is a major bottleneck for specialized applications in multimodal and language generation where annotation is costly. A principled method to select optimal source tasks for transfer, based on intrinsic task relationships rather than intuition, is critically needed to reduce labeling burdens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tasks can be organized into a hierarchical taxonomy based on shared latent skills, and that pre-finetuning a model on a data-rich 'taxonomic neighbor' will yield more label-efficient fine-tuning on a data-scarce target task than pre-finetuning on a randomly selected or more distant task.
proposals.method: We will construct a task taxonomy by embedding tasks using features from a frozen foundation model (e.g., CLIP or DINOv2 for vision, BERT for language) applied to their unlabeled data distributions, measuring similarity via Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA). This hierarchical clustering defines the task manifold. We will then adapt the 'Bridge-Coder' staged transfer framework: first pre-finetune a base model (e.g., LLaMA or a Vision Transformer) on a selected anchor task high in the taxonomy, then fine-tune on the low-resource target task. We will rigorously evaluate the tradeoff between task proximity and transfer efficiency across image-text and text-generation benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, data-driven framework for selecting pre-finetuning tasks, moving beyond ad-hoc choices. It will deliver a measurable reduction in labeled data requirements for fine-tuning on novel tasks, validated across modalities. The resulting task taxonomy and analysis will offer insights into the compositional structure of machine learning problems.
proposals.novelty: While task similarity has been explored, the explicit construction of a hierarchical task taxonomy to guide a staged pre-finetuning pipeline is novel. This approach formalizes the biological analogy of phylogenetics for neural network transfer, shifting the focus from labeling the target to understanding the broader task ecosystem.

proposals.title: Continual Meta-Prefinetuning: Accumulating Few-Shot Learning Strategies Across Task Families
proposals.motivation: Current meta-learning models are trained in a single batch on a static set of tasks, limiting their ability to sequentially acquire new few-shot learning skills without catastrophic forgetting of prior meta-knowledge. In real-world scenarios, new task families (e.g., new visual domains, specialized text genres) emerge continually, each with only sparse labeled data. A method to accumulate and preserve meta-representations over time is crucial for building lifelong, data-efficient learners.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying continual learning techniques, such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) or generative rehearsal over latent tokens, to the meta-optimization process, a model can sequentially pre-finetune on distinct few-shot task families while protecting and refining the core representations and adaptation strategies necessary for rapid future learning.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage, modality-agnostic framework. First, a base model (e.g., a multimodal encoder) undergoes standard meta-pre-training (e.g., MAML) on an initial task family. For each new family, we perform few-shot meta-prefinetuning while applying EWC to constrain changes to parameters identified as critical for the meta-loss, or we use a small generative model to rehearse synthetic few-shot episodes from previous families in a compressed token space. The process iterates, with the protected 'meta-parameters' becoming increasingly general for few-shot adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel continual meta-learning algorithm that enables stable, sequential acquisition of few-shot learning capabilities. It will provide empirical evidence on benchmark splits of how protecting meta-knowledge differs from protecting task-specific knowledge, advancing the field towards more practical, expandable few-shot learners.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is repurposing continual learning not to remember specific tasks, but to remember *how to learn* (the meta-strategy) from previous task structures, a problem largely unaddressed in pre-2026 meta-learning research which treats the meta-training task distribution as static.

proposals.title: Task-Agnostic Representation Learning via Contrastive Self-Generated Proxies
proposals.motivation: Current pre-finetuning and intermediate training paradigms rely heavily on human-curated datasets with explicit task labels (e.g., GLUE, ImageNet), which are expensive to scale and may bias models towards narrow task families. A method that learns robust, transferable representations directly from raw, unlabeled data by inferring underlying data-generating processes would be more scalable and general. This is crucial for adapting large multimodal models to new domains where labeled data is scarce, aligning with the researcher's focus on efficient transfer and modality-specific challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: A contrastive objective, where a model learns to discriminate between plausible and implausible self-generated data transformations, can serve as an effective pre-finetuning signal that encodes generalizable structural and semantic knowledge, leading to superior performance on diverse downstream tasks compared to standard pre-training or labeled pre-finetuning.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated pipeline to generate a diverse set of proxy tasks by applying structured transformations to unlabeled image-text pairs. For text, transformations include syntactic scrambling, semantic role corruption, and style injection; for images, transformations include incoherent patch shuffling and implausible object compositing. We will train a multimodal encoder (e.g., a vision-language transformer) using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) where the positive pair is an original sample and a 'plausible' transformation, and negatives are 'implausible' ones. The training objective is to maximize similarity within plausible pairs, forcing the model to learn latent rules of coherence across modalities without explicit labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, label-free paradigm for representation learning that bridges self-supervised pre-training and task-aware fine-tuning. It will provide empirical evidence on large-scale datasets (e.g., LAION) that learning to identify data-generating processes outperforms learning to reconstruct or predict masked tokens for downstream transfer. The resulting models are expected to show enhanced robustness and sample efficiency in few-shot learning scenarios across NLP and vision tasks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is framing pre-finetuning as learning to distinguish plausible from implausible data states via self-generated proxies, an approach inspired by ecological null models, rather than relying on human-defined tasks or generic reconstruction. This moves beyond existing contrastive methods like SimCLR or CLIP by generating and leveraging a spectrum of *semantic* and *structural* transformations as explicit task proxies within a single training objective.

proposals.title: Anchoring General Knowledge: Contrastive Pre-Finetuning for Efficient and Robust Adaptation of Large Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Massive generative models like GPT-3 and T5 are expensive to fully fine-tune for new tasks, and standard pre-finetuning on narrow target data often leads to catastrophic forgetting of core capabilities. This degradation undermines the versatility that makes these models valuable, creating a critical trade-off between efficient adaptation and knowledge preservation that current methods do not adequately address.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a mixed-data stream combined with a contrastive loss that anchors hidden representations of general knowledge examples to their pre-update states will enable effective pre-finetuning while significantly mitigating catastrophic forgetting, preserving the model's foundational reasoning and linguistic abilities.
proposals.method: We propose Contrastive Pre-Finetuning (CPF), a two-stage method. First, we create a mixed dataset by interleaving target-domain examples (e.g., code, molecules) with a curated subset of the model's original pretraining data (e.g., C4, Wikipedia). Second, we train the model with a combined loss: a standard autoregressive loss on the target data and a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) applied to intermediate layer representations. This contrastive loss pulls the updated model's representations of general knowledge examples closer to their frozen pre-update embeddings, acting as an anchor. We will experiment with different layers and scaling for the contrastive component.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, computationally efficient pre-finetuning paradigm that explicitly preserves general capabilities. It will provide empirical evidence on the effectiveness of representation anchoring for large models and deliver a practical method for safely adapting foundational models to new domains like programming or scientific text without sacrificing their core utility.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is used in pretraining and representation learning, its application as a regularizer during the pre-finetuning of massive generative models to explicitly anchor and preserve pre-existing knowledge is novel. This moves beyond simple data mixing or weight regularization by directly constraining the model's internal representations.

proposals.title: Prime-Boost Learning: Domain-Specific Priming for Efficient Adaptation of Large Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning large generative models (e.g., T5, GPT-3) on specific downstream tasks is data-hungry and can lead to catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge. This is especially problematic for specialized domains like science or finance, where high-quality labeled data is scarce. A method to make these models more sample-efficient and performant for domain-specific tasks is a critical open problem in pre-2026 NLP and multimodal research.
proposals.hypothesis: A heterogeneous, multi-objective pre-finetuning ('priming') stage on broad, unlabeled domain data will reconfigure a large generative model's representations, making subsequent task-specific fine-tuning ('boosting') significantly more data-efficient and effective, analogous to the immunological prime-boost strategy.
proposals.method: We will adapt a large pre-trained model (e.g., T5-XXL or a decoder-only LLM) with a curated priming stage. This involves multi-task self-supervised pre-finetuning on a target domain corpus (e.g., scientific papers) using diverse objectives like span corruption, sentence permutation, and contrastive loss over multimodal tokens (text and extracted visual tokens from figures). The subsequent boost stage will involve standard fine-tuning on specific downstream tasks (e.g., scientific QA, molecule captioning) within the primed domain, with rigorous evaluation of data efficiency and final performance against baselines using direct fine-tuning and standard multi-task learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, biologically-inspired framework for domain adaptation of large generative models, demonstrating improved data efficiency and peak performance. It will yield a systematic analysis of how priming objectives reshape model representations for a domain and establish best practices for the 'pre-finetuning' stage for models beyond the scale of BART.
proposals.novelty: While pre-finetuning exists for encoder models like BERT, its application to large *generative* architectures (T5/GPT) for domain priming is unexplored. The explicit analogy to prime-boost vaccination and the design of a heterogeneous, multi-objective priming phase using multimodal tokens distinguishes it from simple continued pre-training or standard multi-task learning.

proposals.title: Scaling Pre-Finetuning: A Systematic Study on Large Generative Models for Multimodal Tasks
proposals.motivation: Pre-finetuning, an intermediate training stage between pre-training and task-specific fine-tuning, has shown promise for smaller models like BART, improving adaptation efficiency and performance. However, its effectiveness and scaling properties for modern, large-scale generative models (e.g., T5, GPT-3, or decoder-only LLMs) remain unexplored. This gap is critical as deploying these massive models for diverse downstream tasks, especially in multimodal (image-text) and cross-modal (text-to-code) generation, is computationally prohibitive without efficient adaptation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed pre-finetuning stage, using a curriculum of diverse but related generative tasks, will significantly improve the sample efficiency and final performance of large T5 and GPT-style models on target downstream tasks, compared to direct fine-tuning from the pre-trained checkpoint.
proposals.method: We will select large-scale base models (e.g., T5-XXL, GPT-NeoX) and design a multi-stage pre-finetuning protocol. First, we will curate a mixture of datasets spanning related generative objectives (e.g., visual question answering, dense captioning, code generation, and text infilling) to construct a 'pre-finetuning' stage. We will systematically vary the composition, order (curriculum), and duration of this stage. The models will then be fine-tuned on specific target benchmarks (e.g., image captioning, text-to-SQL). We will compare against baselines of direct fine-tuning and multi-task learning, measuring performance, convergence speed, and robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical analysis of pre-finetuning at scale for modern generative architectures, establishing best practices for data mixture and staging. It will deliver open-source, pre-finetuned checkpoints for T5 and GPT-style models that serve as better starting points for community research. The findings will advance efficient adaptation methodologies for large models, reducing the cost of specializing them for new domains.
proposals.novelty: While pre-finetuning has been studied for encoder-decoder models like BART, applying and rigorously evaluating this paradigm for much larger, decoder-only GPT-style models and dense encoder-decoder models like T5 is novel. Furthermore, exploring its application in a multimodal context, bridging image and text tokens, extends the concept beyond pure language.

proposals.title: Dynamic Gradient Surgery for Continual Pre-Finetuning of Multimodal Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Foundation models are increasingly pre-finetuned on diverse, sequentially arriving multimodal tasks (e.g., image-text, code, molecule generation). Standard continual learning methods, like rehearsal, primarily combat catastrophic forgetting but do not actively manage interference between the gradients of new and old tasks during optimization. This can lead to suboptimal convergence and negative forward transfer, where learning a new task degrades performance on unrelated past tasks, wasting compute and data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically applying gradient surgery between the gradients of a new task and the consolidated gradients computed from a rehearsal memory will enable more efficient and stable continual pre-finetuning, simultaneously mitigating catastrophic forgetting and negative forward transfer.
proposals.method: We will adapt a large pre-trained multimodal model (e.g., a vision-language transformer) for a stream of diverse tasks. For each new task batch, we will compute its gradient. Concurrently, we will sample a batch from a fixed-size episodic memory storing past task examples and compute a consolidated gradient representing the direction to preserve past knowledge. We will then apply a gradient surgery algorithm (PCGrad or GradVac) to project the new task gradient onto a direction minimally conflicting with the consolidated rehearsal gradient before updating the model. This process will be repeated sequentially across tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm for continual model adaptation, demonstrating improved stability and efficiency over standard rehearsal or static multi-task gradient surgery. It will provide empirical evidence and a practical framework for integrating dynamic task sequences into foundation models without performance degradation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the dynamic, sequential application of gradient conflict mitigation specifically within a continual *pre-finetuning* scenario, synergizing rehearsal-based memory with gradient surgery to address both forgetting and forward transfer, unlike static multi-task or purely rehearsal-based approaches.

proposals.title: Meta-Prefinetuning: Learning to Adapt for Efficient Few-Shot Multimodal Task Transfer
proposals.motivation: Current large multimodal models require extensive, task-specific fine-tuning to adapt to new domains, which is data-inefficient and computationally costly. This limits their application in low-resource scenarios, such as specialized scientific image-text tasks or niche programming languages. A method that enables rapid adaptation from minimal examples is crucial for democratizing access to advanced AI capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing the pre-finetuning stage as a meta-learning problem will explicitly optimize a model's internal representations for fast adaptation, leading to superior few-shot performance on downstream tasks compared to standard pre-finetuning or in-context learning.
proposals.method: We will adapt a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework for the pre-finetuning stage of a multimodal encoder (e.g., a vision-language model). During pre-finetuning, the model will be exposed to a distribution of related tasks (e.g., different visual QA or code generation tasks) and optimized such that a few gradient steps on a held-out 'support set' for a new task lead to good performance on a 'query set'. We will specifically investigate the transfer of this meta-learned initialization from high-resource to low-resource modalities, building on insights from Bridge-Coder and visual tokenization trade-offs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm that bridges meta-learning and large-scale pre-finetuning, providing a concrete algorithm for learning highly adaptable multimodal representations. It will deliver empirical benchmarks demonstrating significant gains in few-shot learning efficiency across image, text, and code generation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning is well-studied and pre-finetuning is common, explicitly unifying them to create a 'meta-prefinetuned' model for multimodal few-shot transfer is a novel synthesis. The approach moves beyond using meta-learning on small models or treating pre-finetuning as simple multi-task learning.

proposals.title: Task-Clustered Curriculum Learning for Scalable Mixture-of-Experts Pre-Finetuning
proposals.motivation: Pre-finetuning on diverse tasks is a promising path to more general and capable foundation models, but scaling to thousands of tasks risks catastrophic interference and inefficient compute. Existing methods like multi-task learning or sequential fine-tuning struggle with negative transfer and fail to dynamically allocate model capacity based on task relationships. A scalable, structured approach is needed to manage this complexity and unlock the potential of massive, heterogeneous task collections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model, where experts are initialized and trained via a curriculum guided by task clusters, will enable stable, scalable pre-finetuning by minimizing interference and promoting efficient specialization and sharing across related tasks.
proposals.method: We will first generate task embeddings using instruction and data prototypes, then cluster tasks. We initialize a sparse MoE transformer where each expert sub-network is initially assigned to a task cluster. Training follows a two-stage curriculum: 1) Isolated per-cluster training with a task-aware router, freezing other experts to force specialization. 2) Progressive unfreezing and introduction of a softened, gated routing mechanism to allow controlled cross-cluster knowledge transfer, building shared representations. The router will be conditioned on the task embedding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for scaling pre-finetuning orders of magnitude beyond current limits. It will yield insights into the structure of multi-task spaces and demonstrate how curriculum learning can stabilize MoE training. The resulting model will serve as a stronger, more adaptable base for downstream specialization.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of task clustering with a *curriculum-driven* MoE training strategy, moving beyond static routing. This explicitly structures the learning process to first enforce specialization based on task ontology before carefully introducing sharing, a non-obvious but critical step for stability at scale.

proposals.title: Iterative Conditional Fine-Tuning: One-Task Pre-Finetuning for Robust Model Specialization
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale pre-training paradigms emphasize breadth across tasks and modalities, but deployment often requires extreme specialization on a single, massive dataset (e.g., a proprietary codebase or a comprehensive medical image archive). Standard fine-tuning on such a dataset is a single-pass procedure that may converge to a suboptimal, brittle basin in the loss landscape. Inspired by metabolic pathway optimization in systems biology, where repeated exposure to a single substrate under varied conditions yields a highly efficient state, we investigate whether an analogous iterative, conditionally varied fine-tuning process can produce a superior, more robustly specialized model.
proposals.hypothesis: Repeatedly fine-tuning a large pre-trained model (e.g., a dense or mixture-of-experts Transformer) on the same single-task dataset, while systematically varying conditioning factors such as learning rate schedules, noise injection, or data subset sampling, will drive the model's parameters into a more optimal and generalizable region of the task-specific loss landscape, serving as a better 'one-task pre-finetuned' initialization for final deployment than standard fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will adapt a large pre-trained model (e.g., CodeLlama or a Vision Transformer) and perform iterative fine-tuning cycles on a single large-scale dataset (e.g., the Stack code corpus or MIMIC-CXR). Each cycle will apply a distinct conditioning strategy from a predefined set, including cyclical learning rates (e.g., 1cycle policy), stochastic depth or dropout variations, and modality-specific token masking strategies informed by recent visual tokenization tradeoff research. We will rigorously evaluate the resulting checkpoints on held-out validation data, using performance, robustness to distribution shifts, and loss landscape geometry (via Hessian-based metrics) to guide the selection of the final 'pre-finetuned' model for a downstream target task.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel 'depth over breadth' paradigm for model specialization, providing a practical recipe for one-task pre-finetuning. It will yield insights into how iterative optimization under controlled variation shapes model representations and robustness, bridging concepts from systems biology and machine learning. The resulting models are expected to show improved performance and sample efficiency in final target task fine-tuning compared to standard baselines.
proposals.novelty: While iterative fine-tuning and curriculum learning exist, applying systematic conditional variation over cycles on a *single* dataset as a form of beneficial pre-finetuning is unexplored. This directly inverts the multi-task pre-training logic, focusing the scaling effort on depth of optimization for a singular task, an idea adapted from metabolic engineering.

proposals.title: SkillBank: Pre-Training Compact Models for External Skill Retrieval and Composition
proposals.motivation: Deploying capable models on edge devices is hindered by the fundamental trade-off between parameter count and knowledge capacity. Current tiny models fail on complex tasks because they must internally store both procedural knowledge and factual data. A new paradigm is needed to decouple these functions, enabling robust performance from extremely compact architectures suitable for widespread on-device use.
proposals.hypothesis: A very small transformer model can be effectively pre-trained to master the meta-skills of retrieving, sequencing, and applying skill modules from an external, compressed library curated by a large foundation model, thereby achieving task performance far exceeding its parameter-limited internal knowledge capacity.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, multimodal framework. First, a large teacher model (e.g., LLaMA) distills diverse tasks into a library of discrete, executable skill tokens representing fundamental operations in vision, language, and reasoning. Second, a compact student model (<100M params) is pre-trained with a novel objective that combines next-token prediction with a 'skill pointer' loss. This loss trains the model to output retrieval keys for the skill bank and correctly sequence these external skill tokens over multiple steps to solve a given input problem, rather than generating all knowledge internally.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel pre-training paradigm for compact models focused on skill orchestration rather than knowledge memorization. It will provide a concrete method for creating sub-100M parameter models capable of complex, multi-step reasoning by leveraging a shared skill bank, significantly advancing the frontier of on-device AI. We will release benchmarks for external skill-based reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While retrieval-augmented generation exists for facts, pre-training a model explicitly for the lifelong meta-task of dynamic skill retrieval and composition from a compressed, foundational skill library is unexplored. This moves beyond simple prompt retrieval or mixture-of-experts to a fundamental rethinking of the compact model's role.

proposals.title: EmbryoNet: Meta-Optimizing Foundational Weights for Efficient Tiny Model Specialization
proposals.motivation: Deploying capable neural networks on edge devices requires ultra-small models (<10M parameters), but training such models from scratch leads to poor performance due to limited capacity. While transfer learning from large pre-trained models exists, the architectural mismatch and parameter inefficiency for tiny targets remain significant bottlenecks. A method to create a universal, high-quality starting point for all tiny models would dramatically improve their final performance and adaptation efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, general-purpose 'model embryo,' whose weights are meta-optimized on a broad multimodal corpus to instill fundamental latent capabilities, can serve as a superior initialization for any subsequent ultra-small model, leading to faster and more data-efficient specialization than training from scratch or distilling from a mismatched large model.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage 'embryogenesis' process. First, we meta-train a tiny transformer-based embryo model (<10M params) using a curriculum over a diverse corpus of image-text pairs and code, employing algorithms like MAML or Reptile to optimize for rapid adaptation. Second, for deployment, we take this fixed embryo and perform light 'post-natal' fine-tuning, potentially using parameter-efficient methods like adapters or prompt tuning, on specific downstream tasks (e.g., visual QA, molecule property prediction). We will rigorously compare against training from scratch and standard pre-training & distillation baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework and a publicly released 'EmbryoNet' checkpoint for efficient tiny model development. It will advance the theoretical understanding of how fundamental capabilities can be embedded in minimal parameter budgets, directly addressing the compression-generation tradeoff in resource-constrained settings.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in framing tiny model creation as a developmental process with a distinct, meta-learned embryonic phase, moving beyond simple distillation or one-shot NAS. It explicitly optimizes a minimal network for maximal future adaptability, a concept not explored in standard pre-training paradigms.

proposals.title: Inverse Distillation: Multi-Task Learning as a Post-Compression Recovery Mechanism for Efficient Generalist Models
proposals.motivation: Creating small, efficient models that retain the broad capabilities of large foundation models is a critical challenge in pre-2026 AI. Standard knowledge distillation compresses capability but often incurs irreversible loss in task synergy and generalization. Multi-task learning (MTL) is typically used as a primary training method, but its potential as a corrective tool after compression is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying multi-task learning *after* distilling a large model into a smaller one will act as a recovery mechanism, not only mitigating compression loss but also enhancing the small model's resilience to negative transfer and inter-task synergy beyond the level of its teacher model.
proposals.method: We will first distill a large, multi-modal encoder (e.g., a vision-language model) into a smaller transformer via task-agnostic pre-finetuning on a broad dataset, establishing a compressed baseline. Then, we will apply a controlled multi-task learning stage on a curated set of tasks spanning image classification, VQA, and text generation. We will analyze performance trajectories, attention patterns, and gradient conflicts, comparing against ablated models trained with MTL-only or distillation-only regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel pipeline for building efficient generalist models and deliver new insights into the dynamics of knowledge recovery post-compression. It will establish whether MTL can uniquely reorganize a compressed model's representations to improve task synergy and robustness.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is repositioning multi-task learning from a primary training method to a post-compression corrective and boosting stage—an 'inverse' sequence to standard practice. This inverts the typical role of MTL and fuses it with distillation in a non-obvious, sequential manner.

proposals.title: Dual-Explicit: Mechanistic Distillation via Structured Intermediate Pre-Finetuning
proposals.motivation: Model compression via knowledge distillation often fails to transfer the teacher's robust, compositional reasoning, leading to poor generalization on novel or out-of-distribution tasks. Pre-finetuning on synthetic data helps, but standard approaches only mimic final outputs, missing the teacher's implicit intermediate processing steps. This gap is critical for deploying reliable, smaller models in multimodal and instruction-following settings where reasoning is key.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-finetuning a student model on a synthetic curriculum that explicitly includes structured intermediate representations (e.g., reasoning traces, decomposed instructions) from a teacher will lead to superior compression, enabling the student to internalize the teacher's mechanistic strategies and achieve stronger generalization than standard pre-finetuning or distillation.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage pipeline. First, a large multimodal teacher (e.g., a vision-language model) generates a synthetic dataset, including for each input both the final output and explicit intermediate tokens representing structured reasoning steps or saliency maps. Second, we design a pre-finetuning curriculum for a smaller student model (e.g., a compressed transformer) where initial stages emphasize predicting these intermediate representations, later stages blend intermediate and final target prediction, and final stages focus on the end task. The student's architecture will be modified with auxiliary heads to predict the structured intermediates, using a combined loss over intermediate and final tokens.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for mechanistic knowledge transfer in model compression, demonstrating that explicit intermediate supervision during pre-finetuning is more effective than final-output-only supervision. We will provide empirical evidence across vision-language and instruction-following benchmarks showing improved robustness and compositional generalization in compressed models.
proposals.novelty: While pre-finetuning on synthetic data is known, forcing the student to explicitly model the teacher's implicit intermediate reasoning structures as a training objective is a novel and non-obvious extension. This moves beyond mimicking outputs to distilling internal computational mechanisms.

proposals.title: Dual: Pre-Finetuning as a Task-Agnostic Compression Method for Multimodal Models
proposals.motivation: Deploying large, capable multimodal models (e.g., vision-language models) is often infeasible due to computational constraints. While knowledge distillation and model compression exist, they are typically task-specific, requiring a new distillation process for each new downstream application. A method to compress a large model's broad, multi-task capabilities into a smaller, more efficient form without locking it to a single task would significantly improve accessibility and deployment flexibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel 'pre-finetuning' stage, where a smaller student model is trained on a diverse, multi-task dataset generated by a frozen large teacher model, can effectively distill the teacher's generalist capabilities, resulting in a compressed model that retains strong performance across a wide range of unseen tasks.
proposals.method: We will first use a large, frozen teacher model (e.g., a vision-language model like Flamingo or a unified encoder) to generate a synthetic multi-task dataset spanning image captioning, VQA, and text generation. A smaller student model (e.g., a distilled transformer) will then be 'pre-finetuned' on this dataset using a standard language modeling or masked token objective. Crucially, this stage occurs before any task-specific fine-tuning. We will evaluate the compressed model's zero-shot and few-shot performance on held-out multimodal benchmarks, comparing it to the teacher and to models compressed via standard task-specific distillation.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce 'pre-finetuning' as a novel, task-agnostic paradigm for model compression, distinct from pre-training or task-specific distillation. It will provide empirical evidence and a methodological framework for transferring broad multimodal capabilities from large to small models, enabling more efficient generalist models.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in framing compression as a transfer of generalist ability prior to task specialization, diverging from the standard paradigm of distilling for a single, pre-defined task. It leverages the teacher as a scalable data generator for multi-task 'pre-finetuning,' a concept not extensively explored in pre-2026 compression literature.

proposals.title: UniModal-T: A Token-Unified Transformer for Joint Pre-Training on Video, Audio, and Text
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models often treat audio as a secondary modality, added post-hoc to vision-language systems, which fails to capture the intrinsic, synchronous relationships between audio, visual, and textual streams in video data. A model that internalizes these relationships from the start is crucial for holistic video understanding and generation tasks, such as describing complex scenes, generating soundtracks, or editing video content based on natural language instructions. This gap limits the development of truly generalist AI agents that can perceive and interact with the world as humans do.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transformer model, pre-trained from scratch using a unified tokenization strategy for video, audio, and text, will learn richer cross-modal representations and demonstrate superior performance on downstream audio-visual-language tasks compared to models that incorporate audio later or through separate encoders.
proposals.method: We will design a single transformer backbone that processes a unified sequence of tokens. Inspired by 'When Worse is Better', we will develop a parallel tokenizer stage that compresses raw video frames, audio spectrograms, and text into a shared discrete token space, using vector-quantized variational autoencoders (VQ-VAEs) for vision and audio. Following the 'Bridge-Coder' philosophy, we will employ a masked multimodal modeling objective, randomly masking spans across all modalities and training the model to reconstruct them, thereby forcing deep cross-modal integration. Training will leverage large-scale, unlabeled video datasets with naturally aligned audio and (optional) transcribed speech or subtitles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel architecture and training recipe for end-to-end tri-modal pre-training, 2) a public model and benchmark demonstrating state-of-the-art performance on tasks like audio-visual question answering, text-to-audiovisual generation, and audio-conditioned video editing, and 3) insights into the compression-generation tradeoff when unifying three distinct signal types.
