proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the foundational, co-equal integration of audio from the initial pre-training stage, moving beyond the dominant paradigm of bimodal (image-text) foundation models or late-fusion audio add-ons, and in the application of a single, modality-agnostic transformer over a truly unified token sequence.

proposals.title: Staged Multimodal Training for Long-Context Video Understanding and Generation
proposals.motivation: Current video-language models are limited by short context windows, typically processing only a few seconds of footage. This prevents understanding of long-range temporal narratives, complex actions, and causal dependencies in real-world videos. Advancing long-context video modeling is crucial for applications like detailed video captioning, long-form story generation, and complex procedural reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a staged training approach, which first builds strong spatial-temporal representations on short clips and then explicitly trains to integrate information over much longer sequences, can effectively model long-range video dependencies without prohibitive computational cost.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage architecture. First, we pre-train a base model using a masked token objective on dense visual tokens from short video clips, building on insights from visual tokenization trade-offs. Second, we introduce a new 'Long-Range Integrator' transformer layer that operates on sparsely sampled keyframes or aggregated clip-level features, using efficient attention mechanisms (e.g., sliding window, hierarchical) to model minute-long contexts. This stage will be trained on a curated dataset of long videos with descriptive text narratives.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a model capable of coherently understanding and generating text descriptions for videos significantly longer than current state-of-the-art. It will provide a scalable blueprint for extending context in multimodal models and release a new benchmark for evaluating long-form video understanding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit decoupling and staged optimization of short-clip representation learning and long-context integration, a strategy informed by transfer learning principles but not yet systematically applied to the long-context video problem. This contrasts with simply scaling up existing dense transformer architectures.

proposals.title: Adversarially Robust Video-Language Models via Token-Level Perturbation and Modal-Aware Training
proposals.motivation: Video-language models are increasingly deployed in real-world settings where inputs are often noisy or adversarially manipulated, yet their robustness remains under-explored compared to unimodal models. Existing adversarial training methods for vision or language are not directly transferable to the complex, sequential, and multimodal nature of video-text data, leaving models vulnerable to subtle perturbations that degrade performance. This gap is critical as robustness is essential for reliable applications in content moderation, autonomous systems, and assistive technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a modal-aware adversarial training strategy, which generates perturbations specifically over visual tokens and text tokens during joint training, will significantly improve a video-language model's robustness to input corruptions and adversarial attacks without sacrificing its core generative capabilities.
proposals.method: We will extend a state-of-the-art video-language model (e.g., a Flamingo-style architecture) by integrating adversarial training into its multimodal training stage. Inspired by 'When Worse is Better', we will generate adversarial examples via Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) attacks on the compressed visual token sequence and the text token embedding space. A key innovation is a modal-aware scheduling that alternates between attacking visual tokens, text tokens, and their cross-attention layers, forcing the model to learn robust multimodal representations. The training objective will combine the standard generative loss with an adversarial loss computed on these perturbed inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel adversarial training framework specifically designed for generative video-language models, providing a benchmark for evaluating their robustness. It will yield insights into the compression-generation-robustness tradeoff in multimodal tokenization and produce models more resilient to real-world noise, advancing towards reliable multimodal AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is well-studied in unimodal contexts, its application to generative video-language models with a focus on token-level, modal-specific perturbations is novel. This approach uniquely bridges robustness research with the specific architectural and training-stage considerations of recent large multimodal models.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Video-Language Models: A Study of Pre-training on Massive Web Video Datasets
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws have been pivotal in understanding the performance of large language models, but their applicability to multimodal, especially video-language, models remains underexplored. While models like VideoCLIP have shown promise, they are trained on relatively small, curated datasets, limiting our understanding of how video-language representations evolve with data scale. This research aims to systematically study the scaling behavior of video-language models using orders of magnitude more web-sourced video-text pairs, which is crucial for building more capable and efficient models for video understanding and generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that video-language models will exhibit predictable scaling laws with respect to dataset size and model capacity, similar to language models, but with distinct scaling exponents and saturation points influenced by the visual tokenization efficiency and the modality alignment objective.
proposals.method: We will scale the VideoCLIP architecture, using a vision transformer (ViT) for frame encoding and a transformer for text, trained with a contrastive loss on massive web video datasets (e.g., a filtered subset of YT-Temporal-1B). We will systematically vary the dataset size (from 10M to 1B+ pairs) and model parameters, analyzing performance on downstream tasks (zero-shot retrieval, action recognition). Building on recent work on visual tokenization trade-offs, we will experiment with different spatio-temporal tokenization strategies to manage computational cost and study their impact on scaling efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical scaling laws for video-language pre-training, providing a predictive framework for future model development. It will also yield a state-of-the-art, publicly released video-language model trained on an unprecedented scale, and offer insights into the optimal allocation of compute between visual tokenization, model size, and dataset size for multimodal learning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws are well-studied in NLP, this is a systematic investigation into their form for video-language models, a significantly more complex domain. The study uniquely integrates the analysis of visual tokenization efficiency—a key bottleneck—into the scaling law framework, moving beyond simple parameter and data count scaling.

proposals.title: Inverse Tokenization for Video: Aligning Text and Video in a Compressed Latent Space for Conditional Generation
proposals.motivation: Text-to-video generation is a critical frontier in multimodal AI, enabling applications from content creation to simulation. However, current methods often rely on cascaded image models or direct pixel-space diffusion, which are computationally expensive and struggle with temporal coherence. A more efficient approach, leveraging aligned latent spaces for video akin to those used in text-to-image models, remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inverting the visual tokenization process of a pre-trained, high-quality video tokenizer, we can learn a deterministic mapping from a compressed video latent space to a text-aligned embedding space, enabling high-fidelity and temporally consistent conditional video generation and editing.
proposals.method: We will first train a high-capacity video tokenizer (e.g., a VQ-VAE or VQ-GAN) to compress video into discrete tokens. Inspired by 'When Worse is Better', we will analyze the trade-offs in this compression. Then, we will 'invert' this process by training a transformer-based encoder to map sequences of these visual tokens into the text embedding space of a frozen, powerful language model (e.g., LLAMA). This creates a 'bridge' between modalities, similar to 'Bridge-Coder' but for video-to-text. For generation, we will use this aligned space to condition a latent video diffusion model or an autoregressive token predictor on text descriptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for efficient text-to-video synthesis that operates in a compressed, semantically aligned space, improving inference speed and temporal consistency. It will also provide new insights into the 'inverse problem' of modality alignment for sequential visual data.
proposals.novelty: While text-image alignment is common, applying a deliberate 'inverse tokenization' stage to establish a robust text-video alignment in a compressed token space is novel. This approach directly addresses the efficiency-coherence trade-off by decoupling high-quality visual tokenization from conditional generation in a shared semantic space.

proposals.title: Priming for Plasticity: Engineering Initial Weights via Multi-Task Curricula for Extreme Transfer
proposals.motivation: Current foundation models are pre-trained on massive, often monolithic datasets (e.g., web-scale text), which can bake in undesirable biases and limit adaptability to novel, dissimilar domains. The standard approach of starting from a random or pre-trained checkpoint is a blunt instrument for controlling a model's fundamental capacity for transfer. We need a principled method to engineer a model's initial representational geometry *before* task-specific fine-tuning, optimizing for maximal plasticity and efficient adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: A controlled, multi-task priming curriculum applied to a randomly initialized model can induce an optimal degree of representational collapse, creating a 'Goldilocks' initialization that significantly outperforms both standard random initialization and standard pre-training when adapting to a diverse suite of novel, out-of-distribution tasks.
proposals.method: We will train a transformer encoder-decoder from scratch on a massive, curated set of hundreds of foundational tasks spanning image classification, text entailment, visual QA, and code completion, using a multi-modal architecture that processes images and text via separate encoders fused over tokens. Crucially, we will systematically vary the diversity, order, and mixing ratios of these tasks to create distinct priming curricula. We will then freeze these primed initializations and perform few-shot adaptation on a held-out suite of highly dissimilar tasks (e.g., small molecule optimization, audio generation, robotic control), measuring adaptation speed and final performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of how multi-task priming shapes initial weight geometry for transfer, moving beyond the paradigm of task-agnostic pre-training. It will yield a novel, efficient pathway to create highly adaptable base models, potentially bypassing the compute and bias costs of standard autoregressive pre-training. The resulting 'primed' checkpoints could serve as superior starting points for low-resource domain adaptation.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is treating the initial training phase not as a performance objective but as a direct optimization of the weight space for future plasticity, a concept we term 'controlled collapse'. This inverts the standard pre-training paradigm and directly tests the trade-off between representation specialization and adaptability.

proposals.title: Preconditioning Prompt Tuning with Anisotropic Noise: Leveraging Loss Landscape Geometry for Efficient Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like prompt tuning are crucial for adapting large pre-trained models to downstream tasks. However, these methods can converge slowly or to suboptimal solutions, especially with limited data, due to exploration in flat, isotropic regions of the loss landscape. A principled way to accelerate and robustify PEFT by leveraging the intrinsic geometry of the pre-trained model remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: Injecting anisotropic noise, shaped by the local curvature (e.g., via the Fisher Information Matrix) or parameter variance during a pre-finetuning stage, will guide prompt-based adaptation away from flat minima and towards more generalizable, task-relevant basins, significantly improving convergence speed and final performance.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage algorithm. First, we estimate a preconditioning matrix (e.g., diagonal Fisher, empirical covariance) from the pre-trained model on a small, generic calibration set. Second, during an initial pre-finetuning phase on the target task, we inject noise into the prompt parameters (or other PEFT parameters) sampled from a distribution scaled by this matrix, such as N(0, η * diag(F)^-1). This anisotropic noise will be larger in directions of low curvature, encouraging exploration, and smaller in sharp directions. We will integrate this with established PEFT frameworks (e.g., LoRA, prefix-tuning) and test on diverse vision-language and text generation benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, geometry-aware pre-finetuning algorithm that makes prompt-based adaptation more sample-efficient and robust. It will advance the theoretical understanding of optimization dynamics in PEFT and deliver a practical tool for rapid deployment of large models, particularly in low-resource scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While noise injection and preconditioning are known in optimization, their application to *structure* noise based on a pre-trained model's geometry specifically to *precondition* and *guide* parameter-efficient tuning (rather than full fine-tuning) is novel and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Mitigating Task-Specific Representational Collapse via Layer-Sensitive Regularization
proposals.motivation: Modern large-scale models for NLP and generation often suffer from overfitting and poor out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization, which is frequently linked to representational collapse—the phenomenon where hidden representations become overly similar or lose task-relevant information. Pre-2026, regularization techniques like dropout or weight decay are applied uniformly across all model layers, which is inefficient and may harm useful learned features. A principled, diagnostic-driven approach to target regularization where it is most needed remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: Representational collapse is not uniform across a model's layers but is task-specific, and applying targeted, layer-specific regularization informed by collapse diagnostics will yield superior generalization compared to uniform regularization schemes.
proposals.method: We will first develop a diagnostic suite to quantify representational collapse (e.g., via metrics like CKA, SVCCA, or intrinsic dimensionality) across layers for diverse tasks (text classification, conditional generation, multimodal retrieval). Using this map, we will design and integrate task-aware, layer-specific constraints. For critical layers, we will apply targeted interventions such as adaptive spectral regularization on weight matrices, selective application of ReLU-based penalties like DeepKnee, or auxiliary contrastive losses on hidden states. This creates a feedback loop where diagnostics directly inform a sparse set of interventions, optimized via a multi-task training stage over a broad benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-efficient model training by moving from uniform to diagnostic-guided regularization. It will yield a practical method for improving OOD robustness in generation and NLP models. Furthermore, it will offer new insights into the layer-wise dynamics of representation learning across different task modalities.
proposals.novelty: While collapse analysis exists, using it to drive *sparse*, *layer-specific*, and *task-aware* regularization is a novel fusion of diagnostics and intervention. This contrasts with prior work that either applies global penalties or modifies architectures uniformly, without a data-driven map of layer vulnerability.

proposals.title: Heterogeneous Trust Region Regularization: Architecture-Aware Parametric Noise for Vision and Multimodal Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning large vision and multimodal models (e.g., CLIP, Flamingo) on downstream tasks risks catastrophic forgetting and overfitting, especially with limited data. Existing regularization methods like weight decay or uniform noise injection treat all parameters equally, ignoring the heterogeneous architectural components (e.g., convolutional layers, attention blocks, cross-modal connectors) that have distinct mathematical properties and roles. A method that respects this architectural diversity is needed for more stable and effective adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: Defining structured, component-specific parametric noise distributions, with trust region constraints dynamically adjusted by layer-wise sensitivity, will provide superior regularization for vision and multimodal fine-tuning compared to uniform noise, leading to better task performance and reduced forgetting.
proposals.method: We will extend parametric noise trust region methods by defining distinct noise distributions (e.g., Gaussian, low-rank) for convolutional, self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward layers based on their intrinsic parameter structures. During fine-tuning, we will compute layer-wise sensitivity metrics via gradient flow analysis (e.g., gradient norm per layer) to dynamically scale the trust region radius (noise magnitude) for each component. This will be integrated into a staged fine-tuning pipeline, similar to Bridge-Coder's transfer approach, applying the method to models like Vision Transformers and multimodal encoder-decoder architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, architecture-aware regularization framework for vision and multimodal models, improving fine-tuning robustness across tasks like image classification and VQA. It will yield new insights into the sensitivity of different architectural components during adaptation. The method will be released as a practical, modular library for the research community.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work applying uniform noise, this method explicitly models the heterogeneity of model architectures, tailoring noise to component types. The dynamic, gradient-derived adjustment of trust regions per layer is a novel mechanism for adaptive regularization.

proposals.title: PNTR-VM: A Parametric Noise Trust Region Method for Efficient Fine-Tuning of Vision and Multimodal Models
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning large vision (e.g., ViT, CLIP) and multimodal (e.g., Flamingo, BLIP-2) foundation models is computationally expensive and risks catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained knowledge. Recent work on Parametric Noise Trust Region (PNTR) methods, which inject task-specific noise into parameters to stabilize fine-tuning, has shown promise in language models but remains unexplored for visual and multimodal domains where the optimization dynamics and loss landscapes differ significantly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the PNTR framework to constrain updates within a trust region defined by parametric noise will enable more parameter-efficient, stable, and higher-performing fine-tuning of vision encoders and multimodal fusion modules, outperforming standard fine-tuning and adapter-based methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the PNTR algorithm for vision transformers (ViTs) and multimodal architectures with cross-attention. Specifically, we will define a trust region around pre-trained parameters by injecting Gaussian noise scaled by parameter magnitude and optimize a constrained objective balancing task loss and KL divergence. We will experiment with applying PNTR selectively to key modules (e.g., ViT's MLP heads, multimodal cross-attention layers) and evaluate on downstream tasks including image classification (ImageNet-1k), VQA (VQAv2), and image captioning (COCO).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study and adaptation of trust region methods with parametric noise for vision and multimodal fine-tuning. It will deliver a practical, open-source fine-tuning recipe that improves sample efficiency and final task performance while preserving pre-trained representations better than existing methods.
proposals.novelty: While PNTR is established in NLP, its application to non-autoregressive, patch-based visual models and complex multimodal interactions with separate encoders is novel. The core innovation is tailoring the noise injection and trust region mechanism to the unique structure of vision transformers and cross-modal attention layers.

proposals.title: Measuring Representational Collapse Across Modalities: A Unified Framework for Language and Generation Tasks
proposals.motivation: Recent work, such as 'When Worse is Better', has highlighted the phenomenon of representational collapse in visual tokenization, where compressed representations lose critical information, creating a trade-off with generation quality. However, the extent and impact of this collapse across a broader spectrum of NLP and generative tasks—from text summarization to code generation—remain poorly quantified. Understanding these representational dynamics is crucial for diagnosing model failures and guiding the design of more efficient, high-fidelity multimodal models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that representational collapse is a general phenomenon in transformer-based models, manifesting differently across task types (e.g., discriminative vs. generative) and modalities, and that its severity can be systematically predicted by model scale, training objective, and the intrinsic dimensionality of the task.
proposals.method: We will extend the representational collapse analysis framework to a curated suite of benchmarks, including text generation (e.g., summarization, dialogue), code generation (leveraging insights from 'Bridge-Coder'), and conditional image generation. For each task and model (e.g., T5, GPT variants, vision-language models), we will compute layer-wise metrics of representational geometry, such as the average cosine similarity between token embeddings (the 'collapsed' dimension) and intrinsic dimensionality via PCA. We will correlate these metrics with downstream performance, explicitly testing the compression-generation tradeoff. The analysis will be staged, first within a single modality (text) and then extended over multimodal tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, empirical map of representational collapse across major NLP and generation paradigms. It will yield practical guidelines for selecting model architectures and training stages that optimally balance representation efficiency with generative capability. The resulting diagnostic toolkit will be a resource for the community to audit and improve model representations.
proposals.novelty: While collapse is studied in vision and contrastive learning, a unified, task-aware quantification across diverse generative NLP and multimodal settings is novel. Linking this analysis directly to the transfer of capabilities between high- and low-resource domains, as in code, offers a new perspective on model adaptation.

proposals.title: Flat-Tune: Enabling Extreme Task Generalization via Sharpness-Aware Minimization on Diverse Few-Shot Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current scaling laws suggest that generalization requires training on massive, homogeneous datasets. However, collecting such data for every domain is impractical. This work challenges the necessity of scale by asking if a model can achieve broad generalization by being fine-tuned on a very small, highly heterogeneous set of tasks, a scenario relevant for rapid adaptation to novel, low-resource domains. The key obstacle is catastrophic interference between divergent objectives during fine-tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) to find flat minima during fine-tuning on a small set of maximally diverse tasks will yield a model with superior out-of-distribution generalization compared to standard fine-tuning, effectively inverting the traditional task scaling hypothesis by prioritizing loss landscape geometry over task quantity.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a pre-trained multimodal encoder-decoder model (e.g., a Flan-T5 or a small vision-language model) on a curated 'extreme diversity' dataset spanning text generation, image captioning, code completion, and molecule property prediction. The core intervention is the use of SAM during fine-tuning, which minimizes both loss and loss sharpness. We will compare against standard SGD/Adam fine-tuning baselines and a multi-task learning control. Evaluation will measure performance on held-out tasks from the same diverse domains and on completely novel, unseen task types.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for the role of flat minima in cross-domain generalization and establish a novel, data-efficient paradigm for creating generalist models. It will also demonstrate a practical application of SAM beyond robustness, for synthesizing unified representations from disparate data modalities and objectives.
proposals.novelty: While SAM is known to improve generalization, its application to forcibly unify representations from extremely divergent tasks via few-shot fine-tuning is unexplored. The direct challenge to the task scaling hypothesis using optimization geometry, rather than architectural changes or massive data, is a novel direction.

proposals.title: Knowledge Distillation as a Soft Representational Constraint for Multimodal Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning large pre-trained multimodal models (e.g., vision-language models) on specific downstream tasks often leads to catastrophic forgetting of their broad, generalizable representations. This is especially detrimental for tasks requiring both specialized performance and preservation of the model's original capabilities, such as in-context learning or cross-modal reasoning. A method to constrain fine-tuning to preserve these foundational representations is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using knowledge distillation from the frozen pre-trained model as a soft auxiliary loss during fine-tuning will act as a regularizer, effectively preserving the model's original representational structure while allowing adaptation to new tasks or modalities.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a multimodal encoder (e.g., a CLIP-style model) on a target task (e.g., domain-specific image-text retrieval) while adding a Kullback-Leibler divergence loss between the intermediate layer activations or output token distributions of the fine-tuned model and the frozen pre-trained model. This distillation loss will be weighted against the primary task loss. We will experiment with distilling from specific transformer layers and across modalities (image and text tokens) to identify the most critical representations to preserve, drawing on techniques from Aghajanyan's prior work on token-level analysis and model transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, task-agnostic fine-tuning strategy that mitigates catastrophic forgetting in multimodal models. It will yield empirical guidelines on which model representations are most crucial to preserve for maintaining generalizability. The method will be validated across diverse fine-tuning scenarios, including low-resource adaptation and cross-modal transfer.
proposals.novelty: While distillation is common for model compression, its application as a soft, concurrent constraint during fine-tuning to actively preserve pre-trained representations—rather than transfer task-specific knowledge—is less explored, particularly for multimodal transformers where the interaction between image and text tokens must be maintained.

proposals.title: Staged Pre-training for Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Decoupling and Aligning Dense Retrievers with Multimodal Generators
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems often suffer from a training mismatch, where a generator fine-tuned on retrieved passages is coupled with a retriever that was pre-trained for a different task (e.g., contrastive learning), leading to suboptimal end-to-end performance. Jointly training both components from scratch is computationally expensive and risks instability or collapse, as neither module has developed strong initial representations. A principled, staged approach is needed to effectively combine the strengths of modern dense retrievers and large multimodal generators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-stage training paradigm—first pre-training the retriever and generator independently on large-scale, modality-specific corpora, followed by a focused joint fine-tuning phase—will yield a more robust and performant multimodal RAG system than either end-to-end joint training from scratch or simply using off-the-shelf pre-trained modules without alignment.
proposals.method: We will first pre-train a dense retriever (e.g., a bi-encoder) using contrastive loss on a large corpus of image-text pairs. Separately, we will pre-train a multimodal generator (e.g., a decoder-only transformer) on interleaved image and text tokens, using a next-token prediction objective. For joint fine-tuning, we will employ a multi-task objective combining a generation loss on the final output and a reinforcement learning or gradient-based signal that rewards the retriever for selecting passages that improve the generator's performance, adapting techniques from 'When Worse is Better' for managing the tokenization trade-off.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable and stable recipe for training high-performance multimodal RAG systems. It will deliver empirical evidence on the benefits of staged training for retriever-generator alignment and release a model capable of more accurate and grounded generation in data-intensive domains like open-domain QA and multimodal reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work often uses fixed retrievers or attempts full joint training, our method explicitly decouples the representation learning phases of each component before aligning them, a non-obvious strategy given the prevailing trend towards end-to-end optimization. This leverages Aghajanyan's expertise in staged training and transfer ('Bridge-Coder') to a new, multimodal problem setting.

proposals.title: MARGE-Low: Cross-Lingual Few-Shot Adaptation for Extremely Low-Resource Languages via Multimodal Anchoring
proposals.motivation: Extremely low-resource languages lack the monolingual or parallel text data required for effective adaptation of large language models. This creates a significant digital language divide. Pre-2026, models like MARGE, which are trained for multilingual masked sequence-to-sequence generation using a contrastive objective across documents, offer a promising but underexplored path for transfer from high-resource modalities (like images or high-resource language text) to these underserved languages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by fine-tuning the MARGE model with a novel multimodal anchoring stage, where image-text pairs in a high-resource language serve as a semantic bridge, we can achieve effective few-shot adaptation to extremely low-resource languages, outperforming text-only transfer methods.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune the pre-trained MARGE model in two stages. First, a multimodal anchoring stage will update MARGE using a high-resource image-text dataset (e.g., translated COCO captions) to strengthen cross-modal representations. Second, a few-shot adaptation stage will fine-tune the anchored model on a tiny parallel corpus (e.g., <1000 sentences) between a high-resource language and the target low-resource language, leveraging MARGE's document-level contrastive loss for robust alignment. We will evaluate on text generation and retrieval tasks for languages like Quechua or Tigrinya.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, scalable recipe for adapting large generative models to languages with virtually no web-scale text. It will contribute new insights into the role of multimodal signals as a transfer bridge in low-resource NLP. The resulting model and adaptation protocol will be a valuable resource for language preservation and inclusive AI.
proposals.novelty: While MARGE has been used for multilingual tasks, its application to *extremely* low-resource settings via a deliberate multimodal pre-adaptation stage is novel. This approach innovatively treats visual tokens and high-resource text as an intermediate, resource-rich 'modal bridge'—a concept hinted at in 'When Worse is Better'—before final linguistic transfer.

proposals.title: RetroCM3: Inverting the Causal Masked Multimodal Model for Dense Passage Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Dense retrieval models, while effective, are typically trained with contrastive objectives that require large batches of hard negatives and can be unstable. Concurrently, generative models like CM3 (Causal Masked Multimodal Model) have shown strong in-context learning and text generation capabilities by learning to reconstruct masked spans. This proposal explores a novel, generative paradigm for retrieval that could offer more stable training and richer document representations.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the CM3 training objective to reconstruct the query conditioned on the document, the model will learn high-quality, query-aware document embeddings suitable for efficient and effective dense retrieval.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CM3 architecture, which uses a causal decoder-only transformer over multimodal sequences of text and image tokens. For text-only retrieval, we will format training pairs as `[DOC] <mask> [QUERY]`, where the query tokens are fully masked. The model will be trained to autoregressively reconstruct the query given the document. For inference, we will extract the hidden state of a special `[RETRIEVE]` token prepended to the document as its dense representation, enabling fast ANN search. We will evaluate on standard benchmarks like MS MARCO and BEIR.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new, generation-based training paradigm for dense retrieval that moves beyond contrastive learning. It will provide empirical evidence on the viability of causal masked reconstruction as a retrieval objective and release a new model, RetroCM3, for the community.
proposals.novelty: While CM3 is designed for generation, inverting its objective for retrieval is unexplored. This approach is distinct from prior generative retrieval (which outputs doc IDs) as it produces standard dense vectors from a model trained with a novel reconstruction-from-document task.

proposals.title: Scaling CM3: A Systematic Study of Data and Model Size Scaling Laws for Multimodal Causal Masked Modeling
proposals.motivation: The CM3 (Causal Masked Multimodal Model) architecture has demonstrated strong performance in unified image-text generation and understanding by training on interleaved sequences of visual and language tokens. However, its scaling behavior, particularly the interplay between dataset size, model size, and multimodal performance, remains poorly characterized. Understanding these scaling laws is crucial for efficiently allocating compute to build more capable generalist models, a central goal in pre-2026 AI research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling both the dataset size and the model size for CM3 will yield predictable, task-dependent improvements in multimodal understanding and generation, with dataset scaling being particularly critical for reducing hallucination and improving fine-grained image-text alignment.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled scaling study using the CM3 architecture. We will train a series of models (from ~500M to ~7B parameters) on scaled subsets of a massive, cleaned multimodal corpus combining LAION, Common Crawl, and code data. We will systematically vary the training tokens (e.g., 100B to 1T+ tokens) while holding other factors constant. Evaluation will benchmark performance across core tasks: image captioning, text-to-image generation, visual question answering, and novel compositional tasks requiring cross-modal reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first detailed scaling laws for a causal masked multimodal model, providing empirical guidance for future large-scale training. It will also release a suite of scaled CM3 checkpoints and a rigorous evaluation benchmark, establishing new state-of-the-art results for unified generative models on diverse multimodal tasks.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws are established for unimodal language models, this study is novel in its systematic application to a causal masked model (CM3) that jointly handles image and text generation, exploring the unique trade-offs at the intersection of modality mixing, data diversity, and model capacity.

proposals.title: The Intrinsic Dimensionality of Multimodal and Generative Tasks: A Unified Analysis of Model and Data Complexity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work on intrinsic dimension (ID) has primarily focused on analyzing the parameter space of BERT/RoBERTa models on standard NLP tasks. However, the field is rapidly shifting towards multimodal and generative models, where the complexity of data (images, text, code) and training objectives (generation, translation, optimization) is vastly different. Understanding the ID of these modern architectures and tasks is crucial for predicting model trainability, guiding efficient fine-tuning, and revealing fundamental differences in how models represent information across modalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intrinsic dimension of a task-model pair is systematically lower for generative and multimodal objectives compared to discriminative text-only tasks, and that this ID correlates with the efficiency of parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA across these diverse domains.
proposals.method: We will extend the methodology of Li et al. (2018) to measure the ID of fine-tuning trajectories for a diverse set of models (e.g., T5 for text generation, ViT for image classification, and encoder-decoder models for multimodal tasks) and tasks (including code generation, molecule optimization, and image captioning). For each task-model pair, we will perform random subspace training, fit the dimensionality-response curve, and compute the minimal ID. We will then correlate these ID measures with the performance of structured fine-tuning methods, analyzing how ID varies across modalities, model scales, and training stages.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive map of intrinsic dimensionality for generative and multimodal architectures, providing empirical scaling laws for task complexity. It will offer practical guidelines for selecting parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategies based on measured ID and deepen theoretical understanding of the compression-generation tradeoff in model parameter spaces.
proposals.novelty: While ID has been studied for encoder-only NLP models, its systematic application to the decoder-based and multimodal architectures central to current AI research, and its connection to practical fine-tuning efficiency in these domains, is novel and unexplored.

ideas.title: Dual training on code and natural language
ideas.description: Jointly train the infilling objective on code and prose documents for cross-modal understanding.
ideas.sourceTitle: InCoder: A Generative Model for Code Infilling and Synthesis

ideas.title: Contrastive Pre-Finetuning for Large Generative Models to Preserve General Capabilities
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel pre-finetuning paradigm for models like T5 and GPT-3 that explicitly mitigates catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge. The method, Contrastive Pre-Finetuning (CPF), trains the model on a mixed stream of target-domain data and core pretraining data, using a combined loss function. This loss includes the standard generative objective on the target data and a contrastive objective that aligns the model's hidden representations for general knowledge examples with their pre-update states, anchoring fundamental capabilities. The approach aims to make pre-finetuning of massive models both more computationally feasible per update and more robust, ensuring the model retains its versatility while adapting to new domains or tasks.

ideas.title: Dual-Explicit: Mechanistic Distillation via Structured Intermediate Pre-Finetuning
ideas.description: This method enhances model compression by pre-finetuning a smaller student model using a multi-stage synthetic curriculum generated by a large teacher. The key innovation is that the training data includes explicit, structured intermediate representations—such as decomposed task instructions, step-by-step reasoning traces, or saliency maps—that the teacher implicitly uses. The student is trained to predict both these intermediates and the final outputs, ensuring the compressed model internalizes the teacher's compositional reasoning strategies. This results in a smaller model that robustly generalizes to novel task combinations and demonstrates more reliable out-of-distribution performance compared to standard pre-finetuning.

ideas.title: Investigate the inverse text-to-video generation
ideas.description: Use the learned aligned space for conditional video generation or editing from text descriptions.

ideas.title: Scale model size and training data further
ideas.description: Investigate performance gains from even larger model scales and more diverse instruction-tuning datasets.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Autoregressive Multi-Modal Models: Pretraining and Instruction Tuning

ideas.title: Applying Pre-Finetuning to Large Generative Models via Domain-Specific Priming
ideas.description: This idea proposes to apply pre-finetuning to models larger than BART, such as T5 or GPT-style architectures, by drawing an analogy from immunology's 'prime-boost' vaccination strategy. In that field, a heterogeneous priming step prepares the immune system for a more effective targeted response. Adapted to machine learning, the solution involves designing a 'priming' stage for large generative models using a curated, multi-objective pre-finetuning phase on data from a broad target domain (e.g., science, finance). This domain-specific priming, employing varied self-supervised tasks, would reconfigure the model's foundational knowledge and representations to be more amenable to the domain. The subsequent task-specific fine-tuning ('the boost') would then be more data-efficient and yield higher performance, as the model has already been generically adapted to the relevant linguistic and conceptual space, much like a primed immune system.

ideas.title: Apply self-contained decoding to other models
ideas.description: Explore the use of contrastive decoding in other multi-modal architectures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Autoregressive Multi-Modal Models: Pretraining and Instruction Tuning

ideas.title: Pre-finetuning as Model Embryogenesis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of embryogenesis from developmental biology to neural network scaling. The core challenge is making very small models capable and efficient. In nature, complex organisms don't start from scratch for each new environment; they develop from a zygote through a conserved, pre-programmed embryogenic process that builds a general body plan. Analogously, we propose 'Model Embryogenesis' for AI: before deploying any specific tiny model, we develop a single, highly optimized 'model embryo'—a small network (e.g., <10M params) whose weights are not random but are the result of a meta-optimization or curriculum learning process on a broad, general corpus. This process aims to instill fundamental latent capabilities (akin to a body plan with neural, sensory, and motor precursors). This 'embryonic' model serves as the universal, high-quality starting point for all subsequent ultra-small models. Deployment then involves taking this embryo and performing light, efficient 'post-natal' fine-tuning for specific tasks or domains, dramatically improving final performance and reducing the data/compute needed for adaptation compared to training from scratch or from a standard pre-trained base that is too large.

ideas.title: Apply mixture-of-experts to multi-modal models
ideas.description: Use sparsely activated mixture-of-experts architectures to scale multi-modal models more efficiently.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Autoregressive Multi-Modal Models: Pretraining and Instruction Tuning

ideas.title: Quantify representational collapse for more tasks
ideas.description: Extend the representational collapse analysis to a broader set of NLP and generation benchmarks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Better Fine-Tuning by Reducing Representational Collapse

ideas.title: Dual: pre-finetuning as a compression method
ideas.description: Use pre-finetuning to distill a large model's multi-task ability into a smaller one.
ideas.sourceTitle: Muppet: Massive Multi-task Representations with Pre-Finetuning

ideas.title: Inverse Distillation: Multi-Task Learning as a Post-Compression Recovery Mechanism
ideas.description: This research investigates a sequential pipeline for creating efficient, generalist small models. First, a large model's multi-task ability is distilled into a smaller model via pre-finetuning (compression). Then, crucially, the compressed small model undergoes a multi-task learning stage on a curated set of tasks. The core study is to analyze if this 'Inverse' MTL phase acts as a recovery or enhancement mechanism, mitigating compression loss and potentially yielding a small model that surpasses its teacher in task synergy and negative transfer resilience. This fusion uniquely positions MTL not as a primary training regime, but as a post-compression corrective and boosting tool.

ideas.title: Hypernetwork-Guided Sparse Adaptation from Unlabeled Multi-Task Pre-Finetuning for Few-Shot Learning
ideas.description: This approach first performs self-supervised, multi-task pre-finetuning on diverse unlabeled data to build a robust and general-purpose feature extractor without any task-specific labels. A lightweight hypernetwork is then trained on the very few labeled examples to predict a sparse, task-specific mask and delta weights for the pre-finetuned model's parameters. This method strictly limits updates to a small subset of the network, preventing catastrophic forgetting. The system enables effective adaptation to new tasks with only a handful of labels by combining the strength of large-scale unsupervised pre-finetuning with highly parameter-efficient, label-driven specialization.

ideas.title: Invert the training data construction

ideas.title: Use knowledge distillation as a soft constraint

ideas.title: Solve the inverse problem: generate context

ideas.title: Extreme scale-down: one-task pre-finetuning via metabolic pathway optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Systems Biology and Metabolic Engineering, where complex cellular pathways are optimized for a single output by repeated exposure to the same substrate under controlled variations. The analogous problem in machine learning is whether repeated fine-tuning on one large dataset acts as beneficial pre-finetuning. The adapted idea is to study 'iterative conditional fine-tuning': take a large pre-trained model and repeatedly fine-tune it on the same single large-scale task dataset (e.g., a massive code corpus, a medical imaging archive). Each iteration employs a systematically varied conditioning factor (e.g., different learning rate schedules, noise injections, or subset sampling strategies), mimicking the varying environmental conditions in metabolic optimization. The hypothesis is that this process will drive the model's parameters into a more specialized, robust, and efficiently organized state within the task's loss landscape, serving as a superior starting point (a 'one-task pre-finetuned' model) for final deployment fine-tuning compared to a single pass or standard multi-task pre-training. The 'SCALING_DUAL' dimension is explored by scaling down the breadth of tasks to one, while scaling up the depth of iterative, conditional optimization on that single task.

ideas.title: Assume discrete tokens are not optimal

ideas.title: Challenge necessity of massive pre-training compute

ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Autoregressive Multi-Modal Models: Pretraining and Instruction Tuning

ideas.title: Continual Meta-Prefinetuning for Few-Shot Task Families
ideas.description: This research direction investigates a continual meta-learning paradigm where a model undergoes sequential 'pre-finetuning' phases on distinct but related families of tasks, each with only a handful of labeled examples. It adapts continual learning techniques like elastic weight consolidation or generative rehearsal not to preserve specific task weights, but to protect and accumulate the meta-representations and strategies crucial for rapid few-shot adaptation. The synergy enables the model to become a more efficient and generalizable few-shot learner over time, as it builds upon and does not forget how to learn from prior task structures, unlike standard meta-learning which is typically batch-trained. This creates a pathway for models that can continually expand their few-shot learning repertoire in a data-efficient and stable manner.

ideas.title: Intrinsic dimension across diverse tasks and models
ideas.description: Systematically measure intrinsic dimension for many tasks and model architectures beyond RoBERTa and BERT.
ideas.sourceTitle: Intrinsic Dimensionality Explains the Effectiveness of Language Model Fine-Tuning

ideas.title: Architecture-Aware Parametric Noise Trust Regions for Vision and Multimodal Fine-Tuning
ideas.description: This research develops an extended parametric noise trust region method specifically designed for the heterogeneous architectures of vision and multimodal models. Instead of applying uniform Gaussian noise, it defines structured, component-specific noise distributions that respect the mathematical properties of convolutional layers, attention mechanisms, and cross-modal connections. The system dynamically adjusts trust region constraints based on layer-wise sensitivity metrics derived from gradient flow analysis during fine-tuning. This approach enables effective regularization while preventing catastrophic forgetting, making it suitable for diverse downstream tasks from image classification to visual question answering.

ideas.title: Challenge joint training of retriever and generator
ideas.description: Pre-train the retriever and generator separately before fine-tuning them jointly.
ideas.sourceTitle: Pre-training via Paraphrasing

ideas.title: Contrastive Pre-finetuning via Self-Generated Task Proxies
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that pre-finetuning requires explicit, human-defined task labels. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where scientists infer community assembly rules by comparing observed species assemblages to randomized null models without predefined labels, we propose a method for 'Contrastive Pre-finetuning via Self-Generated Task Proxies'. The analogous solution uses trait-based patterns and co-occurrence to deduce underlying processes. Adapted to NLP/vision, the method involves: 1) Automatically generating a broad spectrum of self-supervised proxy tasks (e.g., semantic coherence checks, syntactic plausibility tests, style consistency) by applying structured transformations to the unlabeled data. 2) Framing pre-finetuning as a contrastive objective where the model learns to identify which transformed instances are 'plausible' versus 'implausible' (randomized or adversarial), akin to comparing an observed ecological community to a null model. This teaches the model a generalized, transferable understanding of data structure and semantics without ever being exposed to a downstream task label, potentially leading to more robust and flexible representations for subsequent task-specific finetuning.

ideas.title: Task-Aware Layer-Specific Regularization via Representational Collapse Diagnostics
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to first quantify representational collapse across a diverse suite of NLP and generation tasks, profiling the sensitivity of each model layer to collapse per task. Using this diagnostic map, we then develop and apply targeted constraints (e.g., specialized regularization, architectural modifications, or training objectives) exclusively to the identified critical, collapse-prone layers for each task. This creates a feedback loop where collapse metrics directly inform a sparse, adaptive intervention strategy, optimizing for generalizability without uniformly penalizing all layers. The result is a more efficient and theoretically grounded method for preventing overfitting and improving out-of-distribution performance across a broad benchmark set.

ideas.title: Apply adversarial training for robustness
ideas.description: Use adversarial examples to make the video-text model more robust to input perturbations.
ideas.sourceTitle: VideoCLIP: Contrastive Pre-training for Zero-shot Video-Text Understanding

ideas.title: Invert the training objective for retrieval

ideas.description: Extend the pre-training to handle longer video sequences and more complex temporal dependencies.

ideas.title: Controlled Collapse via Multi-Task Priming: Optimizing Random Initialization for Extreme Transfer
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training a randomly initialized model on a massive, curated set of foundational tasks, not for performance, but to engineer its initial representational geometry for subsequent adaptation. The core experiment is to vary the diversity and structure of this multi-task priming curriculum to control the degree of representational collapse induced, then test how each primed model adapts to a suite of highly dissimilar, novel tasks. This directly tests the hypothesis that some collapse is beneficial for transfer, while providing a novel, efficient pathway to capable models that bypasses standard autoregressive pre-training and its associated biases.

ideas.title: Flat Minima Search for Extreme Task Diversity
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether a model fine-tuned on a very small number of highly diverse and seemingly unrelated tasks can achieve broad generalization, rivaling models trained on many similar tasks. To overcome the instability and interference expected from such divergent objectives, the fine-tuning process employs Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) to seek flat minima in the loss landscape. This approach explicitly penalizes sharp minima, which are likely to represent brittle solutions that overfit to the peculiarities of any single diverse task. The core hypothesis is that SAM's geometry-aware optimization is the key enabler for inverting the task scaling hypothesis, as it forces the model parameters into regions that are uniformly low-loss and stable across all constituent tasks, thereby synthesizing a robust and general-purpose representation.

ideas.title: Use meta-learning for fast task adaptation
ideas.description: Frame pre-finetuning as meta-learning to improve few-shot fine-tuning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Muppet: Massive Multi-task Representations with Pre-Finetuning

ideas.title: Extend method to vision and multimodal models
ideas.description: Apply the parametric noise trust region method to fine-tuning computer vision and multimodal pre-trained models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Better Fine-Tuning by Reducing Representational Collapse

ideas.title: Anisotropic Noise-Guided Pre-Finetuning for Prompt-Based Adaptation
ideas.description: This approach introduces a pre-finetuning stage where anisotropic noise, shaped by the local curvature or variance of the pre-trained model's parameter space, is injected during prompt or instruction tuning. This noise structure guides the model's exploration away from flat, isotropic regions and towards more relevant, task-aligned basins of attraction in the loss landscape. Consequently, the prompt-based learning process becomes more efficient and robust, requiring fewer tuning steps to achieve high performance on downstream tasks. The method synergistically uses the model's intrinsic geometry to precondition and accelerate parameter-efficient adaptation paradigms.

ideas.title: Continual Pre-Finetuning with Gradient Surgery for Dynamic Task Integration
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel continual pre-finetuning paradigm that combines gradient surgery techniques with rehearsal-based continual learning. The core idea is to apply gradient conflict mitigation (e.g., PCGrad, GradVac) not just statically across multiple tasks, but dynamically as new tasks arrive sequentially over time. A small episodic memory of past task data is maintained for rehearsal, and gradient surgery is performed between the gradients from the new task and the consolidated gradients computed from the rehearsal data. This synergy allows a model to be updated efficiently with new capabilities while actively preserving and aligning them with previously learned representations, preventing both catastrophic forgetting and negative forward transfer during the extended pre-finetuning phase.

ideas.title: Cascaded Skill Infusion for Extremely Compact Models
ideas.description: This approach addresses the capacity bottleneck of tiny models by decoupling knowledge storage from reasoning execution. A large foundation model curates a compressed, retrievable library of fundamental skill modules. A very small model is then pre-trained not to store knowledge internally, but to master the meta-skills of retrieving, sequencing, and applying these external skill modules to solve problems. This enables the deployment of extremely compact models that exhibit robust task performance by leveraging an efficient, shared knowledge base, overcoming the intrinsic limitations of their parameter count.

ideas.title: Invert the retrieval-augmented training assumption
ideas.description: Study a model that learns internal knowledge perfectly, making retrieval unnecessary for generation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Autoregressive Multi-Modal Models: Pretraining and Instruction Tuning

ideas.title: Transfer Learning via Task Taxonomy: Reducing Label Requirements Through Hierarchical Pre-Finetuning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that pre-finetuning for a specific task requires massive labeled data for that same task. Drawing an analogy from phylogenetics, where traits of a new species are predicted based on its position in an evolutionary tree, we propose constructing a hierarchical 'task taxonomy'. This taxonomy would map the relationships between tasks based on shared latent skills or data characteristics. The solution is to pre-finetune the model not on the data-scarce target task, but on a carefully selected, data-rich 'anchor task' that is a close relative in this taxonomy. The model thus acquires a foundational representation tuned to the relevant region of the task manifold. When finally fine-tuned on the target task, the taxonomic proximity ensures high transfer efficiency, dramatically reducing the number of required labeled examples. This approach shifts the labeling burden from the target task to understanding and mapping the broader task ecosystem.

ideas.title: Task-Clustered Curriculum Learning for Scalable Mixture-of-Experts Pre-Finetuning
ideas.description: This system scales pre-finetuning to thousands of tasks by integrating a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with a task-aware routing mechanism and a structured training curriculum. It first clusters tasks based on embeddings of their objectives and data, initializing expert subgroups to specialize on distinct clusters. Training follows a curriculum that begins with isolated per-cluster expert training to prevent interference, then progressively introduces soft, gated cross-cluster connections to build shared representations. This approach ensures stable expert specialization, minimizes negative transfer, and enables efficient scaling by dynamically aligning the model's capacity with the inherent structure of the massive task set.

ideas.title: Leveraging Ecological Succession for Pre-finetuning in Low-Resource Languages
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological concept of succession to low-resource language NLP. In restoration ecology, pioneer species prepare degraded land for later, more complex communities. Analogously, we propose a two-stage 'linguistic succession' pre-finetuning strategy. First, a base multilingual model is pre-finetuned not on random tasks, but on a curated set of fundamental 'pioneer' linguistic tasks (e.g., morphological analysis, part-of-speech tagging, dependency parsing) using data from high-resource languages. This stage aims to create a robust and general-purpose linguistic feature space. This conditioned model then serves as the starting point for fine-tuning on the specific, data-scarce target task in a low-resource language. The hypothesis is that this prepared model, having stabilized on core linguistic constructs, will exhibit improved cross-lingual transfer efficiency and final task performance compared to direct fine-tuning or less-structured multi-task pre-finetuning, much like prepared soil supports richer plant growth.

ideas.title: Measuring and Mitigating Representational Collapse via Sharpness-Aware Fine-Tuning
ideas.description: This research direction integrates Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) with a broad analysis of representational collapse. The core idea is to use SAM during fine-tuning to explicitly seek flat minima that preserve representational diversity, thereby directly targeting the root cause of collapse. We then rigorously quantify the degree of collapse across a wide set of NLP and generation benchmarks to measure SAM's efficacy as an intervention. This creates a unified framework where geometric optimization (SAM) and representational analysis co-evolve, enabling the development of fine-tuning protocols that are both stable and representationally robust.

ideas.sourceTitle: Pre-training via Paraphrasing

ideas.title: Inverse Curriculum: Specialize then Generalize for Robust Multi-Task Learning
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'inverse curriculum' concept from pedagogy, where deep specialization precedes broad integration, to the scaling dimension of machine learning. The analogous solution involves having a learner master a specific skill before learning to apply it in a multi-disciplinary context, which often leads to more robust and transferable expertise. The adapted idea is to study a 'fine-tune then multi-task learn' sequence: first, a model is fine-tuned to convergence on a single primary task, then it is trained on a multi-task objective that includes this primary task alongside related auxiliary tasks. The study will systematically evaluate how this inverse order affects scaling laws, model robustness, catastrophic forgetting, and transfer efficiency compared to conventional multi-task learning or sequential fine-tuning, hypothesizing that initial deep specialization provides a more stable anchor for subsequent generalization.

ideas.title: Use memory banks for efficient negative mining
ideas.description: Adapt momentum contrast techniques from image representation learning to the video-text domain.
ideas.sourceTitle: VideoCLIP: Contrastive Pre-training for Zero-shot Video-Text Understanding

ideas.title: Progressive Overload Curriculum for Pre-finetuning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that randomly mixing tasks during pre-finetuning is optimal. Drawing an analogy from Sports Science, where athletic training uses progressive overload and periodized curricula to systematically build capability, we adapt this to machine learning. The analogous solution involves structuring training intensity and complexity over time to optimize adaptation. The adapted research idea proposes replacing random task mixtures with a dynamic curriculum that sequences tasks strategically. Starting with simpler, broadly useful tasks (e.g., basic language modeling, easy classification) establishes a strong foundation. The model then progresses through increasingly complex tasks (e.g., multi-step reasoning, specialized domains) or tasks requiring greater transfer from earlier skills. This structured progression, akin to athletic periodization, may promote more stable and efficient learning, improve final performance on target tasks, and enhance the model's ability to generalize from its pre-finetuning experience.

ideas.description: Replace the dual-encoder retriever with more complex cross-attention or graph-based retrieval models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Pre-training via Paraphrasing

ideas.title: Apply mixture-of-experts to modality-specific tokens
ideas.description: Use mixture-of-experts routing to efficiently handle different token types (text, image, markup).
ideas.sourceTitle: CM3: A Causal Masked Multimodal Model of the Internet

ideas.title: Apply contrastive learning to retrieval training
ideas.description: Incorporate contrastive loss functions from self-supervised vision to improve the retriever's embeddings.
ideas.sourceTitle: Pre-training via Paraphrasing

ideas.title: Automated Search for Maximal Task Diversity in Pre-finetuning
ideas.description: This research develops an automated framework that, instead of scaling the number of tasks, searches for a small set of maximally diverse and complementary tasks for effective pre-finetuning. It inverts the typical scaling approach by formulating task selection as an optimization problem, where an algorithm evaluates candidate tasks for their diversity and synergistic potential to cover a broad capability space. The core hypothesis is that an automatically curated set of 5-10 highly diverse tasks can match or exceed the downstream performance of training on hundreds of similar tasks, providing a data- and compute-efficient path to foundation model preparation. This enables systematic testing of the diversity-over-quantity principle while automating the previously manual and heuristic process of task curation.

ideas.title: Disentangling and Quantifying the Causal Impact of Specific Representational Properties on Cross-Task Transfer
ideas.description: This research first establishes formal, operational definitions for distinct properties of a learned representation that are often conflated under 'collapse,' such as its intrinsic dimensionality, its class-conditional separability, and its degree of invariance to specific nuisance factors. Using a generative model or controlled network perturbations, we then systematically and independently vary each property in a source model's representation. The transfer performance of these systematically altered models is then evaluated on a carefully curated battery of target tasks with known, quantified dissimilarity from the source task. This allows us to determine which specific representational properties, and in what amounts, are beneficial or detrimental for transfer to tasks with different types of dissimilarity.

ideas.title: Continual Task Embedding Learning with Dynamic Rehearsal
ideas.description: This research direction fuses explicit task embedding learning from multi-task robotics with rehearsal-based continual learning techniques to create a system that learns new tasks sequentially. The core innovation is a dual-memory system: one for task-specific parameters conditioned by a continually learned embedding space, and a dynamic rehearsal buffer that stores not just raw data but also task embeddings and their relationships. This allows the model to not only add new tasks without forgetting old ones but also to reorganize and refine the entire task embedding structure as new tasks arrive, enabling the discovery of shared structure and promoting positive transfer. The result is a model capable of lifelong adaptation where the representation of all tasks, both old and new, co-evolves and improves over time.

ideas.title: Modular Cellular Specialization for Multi-Task Pre-Finetuning
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from developmental biology's solution for scaling functional complexity from a single genome, this idea adapts the principle of cellular differentiation to machine learning. The analogous field is Developmental Biology, where a common stem cell population differentiates into specialized cell types (experts) in response to signals, enabling a single organism to perform thousands of specialized functions cooperatively. The adapted solution is to structure pre-finetuning for scaling as follows: a foundational 'stem' model is trained, and during multi-task pre-finetuning on thousands of tasks, task-specific signals (like morphogens) trigger the growth and specialization of distinct, sparse expert sub-networks from this shared foundation. This creates a Mixture-of-Experts system where specialization is modular and emergent, dramatically improving parameter efficiency and knowledge isolation compared to dense fine-tuning, thereby enabling scalable learning across a vast task space without interference.

ideas.title: Compositional Causal Task Graphs for Systematic Generalization
ideas.description: This approach learns to represent tasks not as monolithic embeddings but as structured graphs composed of interpretable subgoal primitives and their causal relationships. The model is trained with objectives that enforce disentanglement and causal consistency between these primitives, guided by both environmental interactions and symbolic constraints. This allows the system to recombine learned primitives and infer new causal rules to tackle novel, unseen task combinations zero-shot, moving beyond interpolation in a latent space to true compositional reasoning.

ideas.title: Optimal Forgetting for Transfer: Testing the 'Critical Compression' Hypothesis
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that less representational collapse always improves transfer learning. Drawing an analogy from immunology and vaccine development—specifically the concept of 'original antigenic sin' where a perfectly preserved, highly specific immune memory can hinder adaptation to novel pathogen strains—we posit that some degree of 'forgetting' or representational collapse may be necessary for optimal adaptation to very dissimilar tasks. The analogous solution involves designing immune responses (via vaccines) that promote a degree of generalization rather than perfect specificity. Adapted to machine learning, we propose to test the 'critical compression' hypothesis: that an optimal, non-minimal level of collapse in a source model's representations creates a more flexible and adaptable feature space for distant target tasks. The research would involve systematically applying controlled forgetting mechanisms to pre-trained models and measuring transfer performance to highly dissimilar domains, aiming to identify and characterize this putative optimal point of collapse.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Seismic Inversion Regularization for Vision/Multimodal Trust Regions
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending the parametric noise trust region method to fine-tuning computer vision and multimodal pre-trained models. By drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic inversion—where ill-posed problems are solved using regularized trust region methods—we adapt its core solution. In seismic inversion, a trust region framework incorporates regularization (e.g., Tikhonov) to balance data fitting with geological prior knowledge, stabilizing solutions against noise. Adapted to vision and multimodal models, the method will define a compound trust region: a primary parametric noise constraint is augmented with a secondary, task-aware regularization penalty. This penalty could enforce perceptual consistency in feature spaces or preserve cross-modal alignment, ensuring fine-tuning updates are robust and respect the structural priors inherent in visual and multimodal data, thereby improving stability and performance compared to applying the basic noise trust region method directly.

ideas.title: Dynamic Gradient Conflict Detection and Sparse Surgery for Pre-Training Objective Alignment
ideas.description: This approach introduces a meta-learning layer that monitors gradient interactions during pre-finetuning, specifically designed for self-supervised and unsupervised objectives. It employs a lightweight detector to identify only those gradient conflicts that threaten the stability of fundamental feature representations, using a running estimate of representational drift. When a harmful conflict is confirmed, it applies minimal, element-wise gradient surgery to align the update directions just enough to preserve representational coherence. This ensures the model benefits from multi-objective training without the risk of the gradient manipulation itself introducing bias or fragility into the learned representations.

ideas.title: Skill-Graph Guided Curriculum Induction for Robust Pre-Finetuning
ideas.description: This research develops methods to automatically construct a pre-finetuning curriculum that induces a robust and composable skill set in a foundation model, rather than simply selecting tasks. The system models the latent skill space, where each candidate pre-training task is analyzed for the novel, transferable, and robustly evaluable skills it demands. It then optimizes a curriculum—sequencing and weighting tasks—to maximize the model's predicted adaptability to a held-out set of evaluation tasks, directly targeting generalizable capability over narrow proficiency. This approach ensures the automated process builds a foundation for effective downstream fine-tuning on diverse, unforeseen problems.

ideas.title: Learning to Perturb: A Meta-Optimization Framework for Adaptive Noise Generation
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework to learn how to generate noise, rather than theoretically deriving a static optimal form. A noise-generating network, conditioned on model state and data context, produces adaptive perturbations. This generator is meta-trained using gradient-based optimization, where its parameters are updated to improve the validation performance of a base model trained with its generated noise. The approach systematically discovers noise strategies—which may be non-stationary and non-uniform—that are empirically optimal for specific tasks and architectures, moving beyond theoretical assumptions.

ideas.title: Dual: measure dimension of the data manifold

ideas.title: Selective Architectural Regularization via Critical Layer Identification
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all network layers benefit equally from uniform regularization. Drawing an analogy from structural engineering and seismic retrofitting, where only critical structural elements are reinforced to prevent collapse, we propose a method for selective architectural regularization. First, a diagnostic phase identifies the network layers most responsible for learning generalizable, transferable features (the 'critical layers'). Subsequently, targeted regularization constraints—such as specialized loss terms, spectral norms, or sparse connectivity—are applied predominantly to these critical layers. This focused intervention aims to efficiently promote robust, generalizable representations where they matter most, while allowing other layers the flexibility to adapt to specific tasks, leading to more parameter- and compute-efficient models with improved out-of-distribution performance.

ideas.title: Dynamic Sparsity-Gated Layer Constraint via Activation Analysis
ideas.description: This approach replaces the static selection of critical layers with a dynamic, data-driven process. A small, trainable gating network observes intermediate activations and predicts which layers would most benefit from constraint application for each batch or input context. Constraints (e.g., spectral norm, weight decay) are then applied selectively through a sparse, gated mask, allowing the regularization strategy to adapt to different data regimes and model states. This ensures constraints target genuinely problematic representations in real-time, improving robustness without imposing a fixed, potentially misaligned, architectural prior.

ideas.title: Task-Agnostic Continual Adaptation via Dynamic Specialization and Predictive Modeling
ideas.description: This system moves beyond task-based continual learning by operating directly on a non-stationary data stream without task identity. It uses a generative world model to predict future inputs and detect significant distributional shifts, triggering the allocation of new, sparsely activated neural sub-networks for novel patterns. A meta-learned importance measure protects parameters crucial for reconstructing or explaining past data, preventing catastrophic forgetting in a purely online and task-agnostic manner. This enables lifelong adaptation in realistic environments where clear task boundaries do not exist.

ideas.title: Architectural Pre-Shaping via Meta-Prompt Scaffolds
ideas.description: This idea integrates pre-finetuning with prompt-based learning by drawing an analogy from structural engineering. In earthquake-resistant design, control systems like base isolators are integrated from the foundation stage, shaping the initial architecture for optimal dynamic response. Adapted to machine learning, the research proposes to design the pre-finetuning phase to explicitly architect the model's internal representations—installing 'architectural hooks' and creating a latent structure inherently optimized for prompt-based steering. This co-design approach, where initial training prepares the model for prompt interfaces, aims to make subsequent prompt-tuning or instruction-tuning significantly more data-efficient, stable, and powerful, moving beyond sequential application to a unified, pre-shaped paradigm.
